title: From Sounds to Scenes: A Benchmark for Evaluating Context-Aware Auditory Scene Understanding in Large Audio Language Models
summary: Recent Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) have achieved remarkable progress in audio perceptual tasks across individual acoustic layers, including speech, sound, and music. However, existing benchmarks predominantly evaluate these layers in isolation, overlooking the complex contextual relationships that arise when multiple acoustic sources co-occur in real-world auditory scenes. Real-world auditory interpretation requires Context-Aware Auditory Scene Understanding (CASU): the ability to comprehend the holistic scene by integrating sound layers. To evaluate this capability, we introduce the CASU benchmark, which assesses whether Audio LLMs can interpret auditory scenes composed of speech, acoustic events (e.g., announcements), and background environments (e.g., traffic), and reason about the logical relationships between these layers. We propose a scalable pipeline for constructing time-accurate, semi-synthetic audio streams by composing real-world scene sounds with synthetic speech. Building on this data, we design four tasks that probe scene understanding: contextual question answering, entity extraction from the scene, speaker role inference, and counterfactual reasoning where scene is manipulated. Experiments across multiple LALMs demonstrate that effective auditory scene understanding requires integration over all auditory layers, rather than reliance on speech or sound alone, underscoring the necessity of CASU for advancing complex audio understanding in LALMs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the CASU benchmark for evaluating Context-Aware Auditory Scene Understanding in Large Audio Language Models, which assesses how well these models integrate multiple acoustic layers (speech, sound events, background environments) to comprehend real-world auditory scenes. The benchmark uses semi-synthetic audio streams and includes four tasks: contextual QA, entity extraction, speaker role inference, and counterfactual reasoning. Experiments show that effective scene understanding requires integration across all auditory layers.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate speech, sound, and music in isolation, failing to assess the complex contextual relationships that arise from co-occurring acoustic sources in real-world auditory scenes. The authors aim to fill this gap by introducing a benchmark for holistic auditory scene understanding.
AI.method: The authors propose a scalable pipeline for constructing time-accurate, semi-synthetic audio streams by composing real-world scene sounds with synthetic speech. They design four tasks: contextual question answering, entity extraction, speaker role inference, and counterfactual reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple LALMs demonstrate that effective auditory scene understanding requires integration over all auditory layers, not just speech or sound alone.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CASU benchmark underscores the necessity of evaluating context-aware auditory scene understanding, as isolated layer evaluation is insufficient for advancing complex audio understanding in LALMs.

title: Efficient Cross-Scale Invertible Hiding Network with Spatial-Frequency Collaboration and Non-Invertible Mechanism
comment: IEEE TNNLS submitted by Junxue Yang, Xin Liao (https://msf-hnu.github.io/)
summary: Image hiding aims to conceal image-level messages within cover images at the same resolution. Invertible neural networks (INN)-based image hiding has emerged as an important branch. It treats concealing and revealing as a pair of inverse problems on image domain transformation and uses INN's forward and backward processes to address them. Due to architectural constraints, existing INN-based methods suffer from single-scale and single-domain feature extraction and limited nonlinear representation capability, resulting in inferior image quality. To mitigate these limitations, we propose an efficient cross-scale invertible hiding network with the spatial-frequency collaboration and the non-invertible mechanism, termed CrosInv. CrosInv exploits cross-scale and spatial-frequency collaborative features while enhancing nonlinear representation. Specifically, we introduce a cross-scale invertible module that bijectively maps inputs to cross-scale representations. To effectively integrate spatial and frequency information, the cross-scale invertible module employs pixel shuffle, Haar wavelet transformation, and their inverse operations for scale transformation. Furthermore, a non-invertible cross dense module is integrated to enhance the nonlinearity. Comprehensive experiments verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed CrosInv.

AI.tldr: CrosInv is a new cross-scale invertible neural network for image hiding that improves image quality by using spatial-frequency collaboration, cross-scale features, and a non-invertible mechanism for enhanced nonlinear representation.
AI.motivation: Existing INN-based image hiding methods suffer from single-scale and single-domain feature extraction and limited nonlinear representation capability, leading to inferior image quality.
AI.method: CrosInv introduces (1) a cross-scale invertible module that uses pixel shuffle, Haar wavelet transformation, and their inverse operations for scale transformation; (2) spatial-frequency collaborative feature extraction; and (3) a non-invertible cross dense module to enhance nonlinearity.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments verify the effectiveness and superiority of CrosInv over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed cross-scale invertible hiding network with spatial-frequency collaboration and non-invertible mechanism effectively mitigates limitations of prior INN-based image hiding methods, achieving improved image quality.

title: OracleAnalyser: Analysing Implicit Semantics of Oracle Bone Scripts through MLLMs with Post-training
summary: With the advancement of artificial intelligence, research on oracle bone scripts has entered a new era. However, existing methods and benchmarks remain largely confined to recognition tasks, overlooking the equally crucial aspect of oracle bone analysis. To address this gap, we propose OracleAnalyser, a reasoning framework for oracle bone analysis based on post-training techniques. Specifically, we fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct through multiple post-training stages and introduce a new preference optimization algorithm, Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO), tailored to the characteristics of oracle bone datasets. In addition, we release both an oracle bone reasoning dataset and an oracle bone preference dataset, and further construct a new benchmark to evaluate models' analytical capabilities for oracle bone scripts. Extensive experiments validate the superior analytical performance of OracleAnalyser, which achieves remarkable results with only 3B parameters, surpassing models with substantially larger scales.

AI.tldr: OracleAnalyser is a 3B-parameter reasoning framework for oracle bone script analysis, fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using multi-stage post-training and a novel Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO) algorithm, which surpasses much larger models on a new oracle bone reasoning benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing oracle bone research focuses predominantly on recognition tasks, while the equally important analytical aspects (reasoning and interpretation) are overlooked. The paper aims to fill this gap by developing a dedicated reasoning framework for oracle bone analysis.
AI.method: The method involves (1) fine-tuning Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct through multiple post-training stages, (2) introducing a new preference optimization algorithm called Stable Focal Preference Optimization (SFPO) tailored to oracle bone datasets, and (3) releasing an oracle bone reasoning dataset, a preference dataset, and a new benchmark for evaluating analytical capabilities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that OracleAnalyser achieves superior analytical performance with only 3B parameters, surpassing models with substantially larger parameter scales on the constructed benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates that targeted post-training and preference optimization can enable small models (3B) to outperform much larger models on specialized oracle bone analysis tasks, highlighting the effectiveness of the approach for domain-specific historical document understanding.

title: Fifty Years of Specification Completeness: What Aviation Certification Tells AI Governance About Epoch Limits, Proof Surfaces, and the Structural Gap
summary:
Aviation software certification has operationalised three structural requirements for governed software systems since 1992: structured governance linkage between governing specifications and operational evidence, context-bounded validity that triggers revalidation when operational context changes, and an objective evidence architecture that defines what proof means and what makes it sufficient. These requirements appear in DO-178C and DO-330 and are enforced through FAA and EASA certification.
  No existing framework requires these structural properties as intrinsic properties of individual AI governance documents. A system prompt, an AGENTS.md file, a governance policy, or a task envelope can be deployed without satisfying any of the three requirements aviation has enforced for three decades. Aviation is the most technically rigorous instance: its standard-setting bodies have acknowledged that their frameworks break down for AI systems, yet none requires these properties of individual governance documents.
  Aviation's structural requirements break down at the system level because AI systems are non-deterministic, but remain transferable at the document level: the governance artifact is a static artifact whose structural properties can be evaluated independently of the stochastic system it governs.
  The paper maps DO-178C's traceability architecture, DO-330's requalification triggers, and DO-178C's objective evidence requirements onto three structural findings: epoch limits on governance document validity, proof surfaces as the revalidation feedback mechanism, and the absence of structural completeness requirements in AI governance instruments. An empirical companion (arXiv:2604.21090) found that 37% of AI governance documents fall below the structural quality threshold. PromptQ's seven-principle framework operationalises these requirements at the governance document layer.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies three structural requirements from aviation software certification (DO-178C/DO-330) — governance linkage, context-bounded validity, and objective evidence architecture — and argues they are missing from AI governance documents. It maps these requirements to structural findings for AI governance and cites empirical evidence that 37% of AI governance documents fail structural quality thresholds, proposing PromptQ as a solution.
AI.motivation: Aviation certification has enforced three structural requirements for governed software for 30+ years, but no existing framework requires these as intrinsic properties of individual AI governance documents. AI governance artifacts (system prompts, AGENTS.md files, policies) can be deployed without satisfying any of these requirements.
AI.method: The paper maps DO-178C's traceability, DO-330's requalification triggers, and objective evidence requirements onto three structural findings for AI governance: epoch-limited validity, proof surfaces as revalidation feedback, and missing structural completeness. It references an empirical companion study (arXiv:2604.21090) and PromptQ's seven-principle framework.
AI.result: Aviation's structural requirements break down at the system level for non-deterministic AI systems but remain transferable at the document level as static artifact properties. 37% of AI governance documents fall below structural quality thresholds per the companion study.
AI.conclusion: The three aviation-derived structural requirements (governance linkage, context-bounded validity, objective evidence architecture) should be operationalised as intrinsic properties of individual AI governance documents. PromptQ's framework offers one instantiation of this approach.

title: Systematic Exploration of 4-Expert Heterogeneous Mixture-of-Experts via Automated Pipeline Search
summary: We present an automated large-scale search pipeline for heterogeneous 4-Expert Mixture-of-Experts (MoE4) architectures within the LEMUR neural network dataset ecosystem. Building on a hand-crafted heterogeneous MoE reference model, we replace manual design with a deterministic code-assembly generator that systematically combines base architecture families drawn from the LEMUR database into MoE4 ensembles, each governed by a convolutional gating network with temperature scaling, mixup augmentation, and cosine-annealed learning rate scheduling. Over a 28-day campaign on an NVIDIA RTX 4090, the pipeline generated 4,463 candidate models across 197 batches, of which 1,021 were evaluated successfully. A critical finding emerged from the campaign: due to alphabetical enumeration via itertools.combinations, the entire explored search space (4.8% of the theoretical 23,751 possible 4-family combinations) is anchored to a single family, AirNet. We characterise this coverage bias precisely, identify the root cause in the generator, and propose a stratified random sampling fix. Within the AirNet anchored scope, ShuffleNet and MobileNetV3 consistently co-produce the highest-accuracy ensembles (mean accuracy up to 0.632), while FractalNet and MNASNet are identified as low-yield families warranting exclusion in future campaigns. The pipeline, analysis artefacts, and corrected generator are released as part of the open-source NNGPT project at https://github.com/ABrain-One/nn-gpt

AI.tldr: This paper presents an automated pipeline for generating heterogeneous Mixture-of-Experts (MoE4) architectures within the LEMUR neural network ecosystem. Over a 28-day campaign on an RTX 4090, 1,021 models were evaluated from 4,463 candidates. A critical coverage bias was discovered: due to alphabetical enumeration via itertools.combinations, the entire search space (4.8% of theoretical) was anchored to the AirNet family. Within this biased scope, ShuffleNet+MobileNetV3 ensembles perform best (mean accuracy 0.632), while FractalNet and MNASNet are low-yield. The pipeline, artifacts, and corrected generator are open-sourced.
AI.motivation: To automate the design of heterogeneous MoE architectures, replacing manual design with a systematic, large-scale search pipeline to discover optimal expert combinations from the LEMUR neural network ecosystem.
AI.method: A deterministic code-assembly generator systematically combines base architecture families from the LEMUR database into MoE4 ensembles, each using a convolutional gating network with temperature scaling, mixup augmentation, and cosine-annealed learning rate scheduling. The pipeline was run for 28 days on an NVIDIA RTX 4090.
AI.result: 4,463 candidate models were generated across 197 batches; 1,021 successfully evaluated. A critical coverage bias was identified: due to itertools.combinations alphabetical ordering, the search space (4.8% of 23,751 possible combinations) was entirely anchored to the AirNet family. Within this scope, ShuffleNet+MobileNetV3 achieved highest mean accuracy (0.632); FractalNet and MNASNet were low-yield.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline is effective but suffers from a systematic enumeration bias. The root cause is identified, and a stratified random sampling fix is proposed. Recommendations include excluding low-yield families (FractalNet, MNASNet) in future campaigns. The corrected generator and analysis artifacts are released as open-source.

title: Self-supervised Garment Dynamics with Persistent Wrinkles
summary: The self-supervised neural garment simulator has become popular due to its high efficiency, good visual realism, and no reliance on training data. However, existing methods greatly simplify the mechanical properties of fabrics, ignoring persistent wrinkles caused by plasticity. Although this simplification allows for modeling of purely elastic material and simple training via energy minimization, the lack of believable wrinkles adversely affects the visual realism. Therefore, we introduce the first self-supervised neural garment simulator that explicitly models persistent wrinkles. This is achieved by a novel physics-inspired loss function, turning learning into a moving energy minimization problem to mimic plasticity. However, this requires learning to use a changing loss function, which causes difficulties in training i.e. the loss function changes during training. To this end, we propose a new physics-inspired curriculum learning scheme where the target material for learning gradually changes from pure elasticity to elasto-plasticity, allowing the loss function and the learnable parameters to jointly converge. Through a comprehensive evaluation, we show that for the first time, self-supervised learning models can generate natural persistent wrinkles, outperforming existing methods on a variety of garments, body shapes, and body motions, according to a range of metrics.

AI.tldr: First self-supervised neural garment simulator that models persistent wrinkles via a physics-inspired loss function with moving energy minimization and a curriculum learning scheme transitioning from pure elasticity to elasto-plasticity.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised neural garment simulators greatly simplify fabric mechanical properties by ignoring plasticity, leading to lack of persistent wrinkles and reduced visual realism.
AI.method: 1) Novel physics-inspired loss function that turns learning into a moving energy minimization problem to mimic plasticity. 2) Physics-inspired curriculum learning scheme where the target material gradually changes from pure elasticity to elasto-plasticity, enabling joint convergence of loss function and learnable parameters.
AI.result: The proposed method is the first self-supervised learning model capable of generating natural persistent wrinkles, outperforming existing methods across various garments, body shapes, and body motions on multiple metrics.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised neural garment simulators can effectively model persistent wrinkles by incorporating plasticity through a carefully designed curriculum learning approach that addresses the challenge of a changing loss function during training.

title: LLM4MTLs: Automated Generation and Empirical Evaluation of Model Transformation Languages
summary: Model transformation languages (MTLs) are domain-specific languages for transforming models conforming to a given metamodel into other models, including textual models such as source code. Developing correct model transformations is challenging, requiring both language-specific and domain knowledge, and motivating the use of large language models (LLMs) for MTL code generation. However, due to limited training data and executable examples, LLM-generated MTL code is often not syntactically valid or semantically usable out of the box. This paper presents LLM4MTLs, an automated workflow for constructing and comparing prompting strategies for LLM-generated MTL code, together with an evaluation suite and an empirical evaluation. The workflow systematically explores prompt constructions combining few-shot prompting, grammar prompting, and helper method inclusion, and evaluates them using syntactic and semantic metrics. We construct an evaluation suite spanning four MTLs (ATL, ETL, QVTo, and the Reactions language) with executable reference scripts and manually written test suites, and evaluate across three LLMs. We find that few-shot prompting consistently improves syntactic quality across all four MTLs while gains in semantic correctness are uneven and language-dependent. For ATL, Pass@1 remains unchanged across all strategies and models, indicating that few-shot prompting improves surface-level syntax more readily than deep transformation semantics. Grammar prompting stabilizes code generation when combined with few-shot examples, but in isolation it can be ineffective or even counterproductive for certain model-language combinations. Including helper methods as a complementary amplifier can also be beneficial. Finally, LLM choice influences syntactic correctness and similarity for certain MTLs, particularly ETL and QVTo, while its influence on semantic correctness remains limited.

AI.tldr: This paper presents LLM4MTLs, an automated workflow for constructing and comparing prompting strategies for LLM-generated model transformation code, evaluated across four MTLs and three LLMs. Findings show few-shot prompting improves syntactic quality consistently but semantic gains are uneven; grammar prompting stabilizes generation when combined with few-shots but can be counterproductive alone; LLM choice influences syntactic correctness for some MTLs but has limited impact on semantic correctness.
AI.motivation: Developing correct model transformations is challenging due to the need for both language-specific and domain knowledge. LLM-generated MTL code is often syntactically invalid or semantically unusable due to limited training data and executable examples, motivating the need for systematic investigation of prompting strategies.
AI.method: The authors construct LLM4MTLs, an automated workflow that systematically explores prompt constructions combining few-shot prompting, grammar prompting, and helper method inclusion. They build an evaluation suite spanning four MTLs (ATL, ETL, QVTo, Reactions) with executable reference scripts and test suites, and evaluate across three LLMs using syntactic and semantic metrics.
AI.result: Few-shot prompting consistently improves syntactic quality across all four MTLs, but semantic correctness gains are uneven and language-dependent. For ATL, Pass@1 remains unchanged across strategies and models. Grammar prompting stabilizes code generation with few-shot examples but can be counterproductive alone. Helper methods can be beneficial as complementary amplifiers. LLM choice influences syntactic correctness for ETL and QVTo but has limited influence on semantic correctness.
AI.conclusion: Few-shot prompting improves surface-level syntax more readily than deep transformation semantics. Grammar prompting requires combination with few-shot examples for effectiveness. The paper provides an automated workflow and empirical insights for constructing effective prompting strategies for MTL code generation with LLMs.

title: Weight-Space Geometry of Offline Reasoning Training
summary: Offline reinforcement-learning losses (RFT, RIFT, DFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) are widely used to distill reasoning from large teachers into smaller students, and are typically compared on downstream accuracy alone. We ask whether they are mechanistically distinct or converge to a similar weight update. Training six methods (SFT, RFT, DFT, RIFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) on identical math rollouts from a single base model (Qwen3-4B) with attention-only LoRA, we analyze the resulting deltas via cosine similarity, principal-angle subspace analysis, linear mode connectivity, and CKA. We observe: (i) SFT, RFT, and RIFT have nearly colinear weight deltas (cosine >= 0.97, top-1 principal angle ~7 deg median over 144 modules) and comparable GSM8K accuracy (87-88%, n=1319; pairwise McNemar p >= 0.15); (ii) DFT diverges further in direction than any reward-weighted method despite using the same data; (iii) Offline GRPO adds a substantial component orthogonal to the SFT direction (~67% globally, up to ~86% in late layers) while staying in the SFT loss basin; (iv) DPO sits in a near-orthogonal subspace, shows a mode-connectivity barrier, and collapses late-layer CKA to ~0.46. DPO also reaches the highest accuracy in our protocol on both GSM8K (93.5%, McNemar p < 10^-9 vs. each other method) and AIME26 (30.0% vs. 3.3-10.0%); its training uses a 10x smaller learning rate than the others (the standard convention), so the update-norm and accuracy gaps reflect loss-function and optimizer choices jointly, and a learning-rate-matched DPO comparison is left for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper compares six offline RL methods (SFT, RFT, DFT, RIFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) training on identical math rollouts from Qwen3-4B with attention-only LoRA. It finds that SFT, RFT, and RIFT produce nearly colinear weight updates (cosine ≥ 0.97) with similar accuracy (87-88% GSM8K). DFT diverges more than reward-weighted methods. Offline GRPO adds orthogonal components while staying in the SFT loss basin. DPO sits in a near-orthogonal subspace with a mode-connectivity barrier and achieves highest accuracy (93.5% GSM8K, 30% AIME26) but uses a 10× smaller learning rate, so conclusions are confounded by optimizer choices.
AI.motivation: Prior work compares offline RL methods on downstream accuracy alone without investigating whether they are mechanistically distinct or converge to similar weight updates.
AI.method: Train six methods (SFT, RFT, DFT, RIFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) on identical math rollouts from Qwen3-4B with attention-only LoRA. Analyze weight deltas via cosine similarity, principal-angle subspace analysis, linear mode connectivity, and CKA.
AI.result: (i) SFT/RFT/RIFT have nearly colinear weight deltas (cosine ≥ 0.97, median principal angle ~7°) and comparable GSM8K accuracy (87-88%). (ii) DFT diverges more than reward-weighted methods. (iii) Offline GRPO adds ~67% orthogonal component globally (up to ~86% in late layers) but stays in SFT loss basin. (iv) DPO sits in near-orthogonal subspace with mode-connectivity barrier and late-layer CKA ~0.46. DPO achieves highest accuracy (93.5% GSM8K, 30% AIME26) but uses 10× smaller learning rate.
AI.conclusion: The six methods are mechanistically distinct despite some sharing similar weight updates. DPO operates in a fundamentally different subspace than SFT-based methods. Results are confounded by DPO's 10× smaller learning rate, warranting future matched learning rate comparisons.

title: Wan-Streamer v0.1: End-to-end Real-time Interactive Foundation Models
summary: We present Wan-Streamer, a native-streaming, end-to-end interactive foundation model designed from the ground up for real-time, low-latency, full-duplex audio-visual interaction. Wan-Streamer seamlessly models language, audio, and video as both input and output within a single Transformer, where the sequence is represented as interleaved visual, audio, and text input tokens together with visual, audio, and text output tokens, coordinated by block-causal attention for incremental streaming. Unlike cascaded interactive systems that rely on separate VAD, ASR, language, TTS, audio-driven animation, or video-generation modules, Wan-Streamer does not rely on external language, speech, avatar, or video-generation modules: perception, reasoning, generation, response timing, turn management, and cross-modal synchronization are learned jointly within one unified model, reducing pipeline latency and error accumulation. To support natural audio-visual responsiveness, we redesign the entire stack around streamability, including causal encoders, causal decoders, block-causal attention, and low-latency multimodal token scheduling, enabling streaming units as short as 160 ms at 25 fps. Wan-Streamer achieves approximately 200 ms model-side response latency and approximately 550 ms total interaction latency when combined with 350 ms bidirectional network latency, supporting sub-second duplex audio-visual communication. These results position Wan-Streamer as a unified, end-to-end, multimodal interactive foundation model for low-latency streaming interaction.

AI.tldr: Wan-Streamer is a native-streaming, end-to-end interactive foundation model for real-time, low-latency, full-duplex audio-visual interaction. It unifies language, audio, and video as both input and output within a single Transformer using block-causal attention and streaming units as short as 160 ms, achieving ~200 ms model-side latency and ~550 ms total interaction latency.
AI.motivation: Existing cascaded interactive systems rely on separate VAD, ASR, language, TTS, audio-driven animation, or video-generation modules, which introduce pipeline latency, error accumulation, and lack cross-modal synchronization. The goal is to build a unified model that jointly learns perception, reasoning, generation, timing, turn management, and synchronization for real-time audio-visual interaction.
AI.method: A single Transformer architecture with interleaved visual, audio, and text input/output tokens, coordinated by block-causal attention for incremental streaming. The stack is redesigned around streamability, including causal encoders/decoders, block-causal attention, and low-latency multimodal token scheduling. Streaming units operate at 160 ms granularity at 25 fps.
AI.result: ~200 ms model-side response latency and ~550 ms total interaction latency (including 350 ms bidirectional network latency), supporting sub-second duplex audio-visual communication.
AI.conclusion: Wan-Streamer demonstrates that a unified, end-to-end multimodal interactive foundation model can achieve low-latency streaming interaction without relying on external modules, positioning it as a promising approach for real-time audio-visual communication.

title: Tensor-Based Batch Fuzzing with Adaptive Perturbation Scaling for Deep Neural Networks
comment: This paper has been accepted by International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE) 2026
summary: Deep neural networks are increasingly deployed in safety-critical domains such as autonomous driving and medical diagnosis, yet their opaque, high-dimensional parameter spaces make it difficult to systematically assess model reliability on unseen inputs. Existing coverage-guided sequential fuzzing frameworks for DNNs inherit a one-input-per-iteration design from traditional software fuzzing and apply uniform perturbation budgets across all input dimensions, limiting both testing throughput (i.e., inputs processed per unit time) and the precision of input-space exploration. We present a new specification-aware batch fuzzing framework with adaptive perturbation scaling that addresses both limitations. Rather than relying on a fixed global perturbation radius epsilon, our approach derives mutation step sizes from specification-defined feasible ranges (the gap between lower and upper bounds) using a shared scale factor. This scaling can be applied either as a global scalar (isotropic) or as per-dimension step sizes (anisotropic), keeping perturbations consistent with the underlying constraint structure. As a result, the fuzzer can explore input spaces with heterogeneous feature scales more effectively across all specifications in the batch. We embed input constraints and output property checks directly into the network as non-trainable layers, yielding a wrapped model that processes B specification instances in a single batched iteration, substantially improving fuzzing efficiency and counterexample exploration. We evaluate our framework extensively on three benchmarks, covering six networks and over 400 specifications across TrafficSigns, Cifar100, and TinyImageNet. Our tensor-based fuzzing achieves up to 40X higher throughput and 4X more violations than the sequential baseline under the same time budget, demonstrating significantly improved effectiveness in specification-guided fuzzing.

AI.tldr: A specification-aware batch fuzzing framework with adaptive perturbation scaling for DNNs that processes multiple inputs simultaneously, achieving up to 40X higher throughput and 4X more violations than sequential baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing coverage-guided sequential fuzzing for DNNs uses one-input-per-iteration and uniform perturbation budgets, limiting testing throughput and input-space exploration precision, making it difficult to assess model reliability on unseen inputs in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: The framework uses adaptive perturbation scaling derived from specification-defined feasible ranges (isotropic or anisotropic), embeds input constraints and output property checks as non-trainable layers into the network, and processes B specification instances in a single batched iteration.
AI.result: Evaluated on three benchmarks (TrafficSigns, Cifar100, TinyImageNet) across six networks and over 400 specifications, achieving up to 40X higher throughput and 4X more violations under the same time budget compared to sequential baseline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed batch fuzzing approach with adaptive perturbation scaling significantly improves fuzzing efficiency and counterexample exploration for DNN specification-guided testing.

title: A Survey on Federated Causal Discovery and Inference
summary: Causal reasoning, which encompasses the discovery of causal structures and the inference of causal effects, is fundamental to data-driven decision making. In practice, data for reliable causal analysis are often distributed across institutions and cannot be centralized due to privacy regulations or communication constraints. Federated learning (FL) addresses this by enabling collaborative analysis without raw data sharing, giving rise to the rapidly growing field of federated causal discovery (FCD) and inference (FCI). However, the interdisciplinary nature of this field and the absence of a comprehensive survey present barriers to entry for researchers. This paper bridges that gap by providing a systematic review through multi-dimensional taxonomies. Grounded in the three core design decisions underlying any FCD solution, namely how structures are learned, how data are partitioned, and what structural knowledge each party obtains, we organize FCD along three axes: methodological paradigm, federation topology, and structural scope. We further examine key practical dimensions, including temporal dynamics, data heterogeneity, missing data, and non-identical variable sets. For FCI, we categorize methods by target estimand (average versus individualized/conditional treatment effects) and by estimation strategy, from classical weighting methods to modern deep generative architectures. Unlike prior works that treat FCD and FCI separately, we formalize their connection as complementary stages of a unified federated causal reasoning pipeline, where FCD supplies the structural knowledge required for valid effect estimation in FCI. Finally, we highlight their shared concerns regarding privacy, communication efficiency, theoretical guarantees, and application domains, and conclude by identifying open challenges for future research.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a systematic, taxonomy-based review of federated causal discovery (FCD) and federated causal inference (FCI), organizing methods along key design axes and formalizing them as complementary stages of a unified federated causal reasoning pipeline, while highlighting open challenges.
AI.motivation: Causal reasoning is critical for data-driven decision making, but data required for reliable causal analysis is often distributed across institutions and cannot be centralized due to privacy or communication constraints. Despite the rapid growth of federated causal discovery and inference, the interdisciplinary nature and lack of a comprehensive survey create barriers for researchers entering this field.
AI.method: The authors develop multi-dimensional taxonomies to systematically organize the literature. For FCD, they use three axes: methodological paradigm, federation topology, and structural scope, grounded in three core design decisions (how structures are learned, data partitioning, and structural knowledge obtained). They also examine practical dimensions like temporal dynamics, data heterogeneity, missing data, and non-identical variable sets. For FCI, they categorize by target estimand and estimation strategy. They then formalize the FCD-FCI connection as a unified pipeline.
AI.result: A comprehensive taxonomy and systematic review of federated causal reasoning methods, covering both FCD and FCI across multiple dimensions, and a formal framework connecting them as complementary stages in a unified pipeline.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights shared concerns (privacy, communication efficiency, theoretical guarantees, application domains) and identifies open challenges for future research, providing a roadmap for advancing federated causal reasoning.

title: Cage-based Texture Transfer with Geometric Filtering
summary: Real-time texture transfer expands the creative horizon for interactive applications, enabling seamless detail projection in scenarios that range from digital character cosmetics to procedural automotive texturing. Yet, its practical application is governed by inherent trade-offs between processing speed and suppression of artifacts. Low-latency transfer methods frequently fail to suppress artifacts, and robust alternatives rely on large-scale models that are costly in training and memory. Our proposed method bridges the gap between efficiency and robustness by using a cage-based geometric filtering method to identify Non-Cosmetic Zones (NCZs) for artifact suppression. While other models are resource-intensive and require multiple days of training on manually annotated datasets, we are able to successfully suppress artifacts and achieve immediate deployment on consumer-grade hardware. Our framework achieved highly efficient runtimes of ~70ms on mobile devices for a ~4.8k triangle mesh.

AI.tldr: A cage-based geometric filtering method for real-time texture transfer that identifies Non-Cosmetic Zones (NCZs) to suppress artifacts, achieving ~70ms runtime on mobile devices for ~4.8k triangle meshes without requiring costly training or large-scale models.
AI.motivation: Existing real-time texture transfer methods face trade-offs between processing speed and artifact suppression; low-latency methods fail to suppress artifacts while robust alternatives rely on large-scale models that are costly in training and memory, limiting practical deployment.
AI.method: A cage-based geometric filtering approach that detects Non-Cosmetic Zones (NCZs) on meshes to identify regions prone to artifacts and suppress them during texture transfer, enabling efficient processing without large-scale model training.
AI.result: Achieved ~70ms runtime on mobile devices for ~4.8k triangle meshes, with immediate deployment on consumer-grade hardware and successful artifact suppression.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method bridges the gap between efficiency and robustness in real-time texture transfer, offering artifact suppression without the resource-intensive training and memory costs of alternative approaches.

title: How Do Developers Maintain and Evolve Their Agents' Instructions? An Empirical Study
summary: Context. Autonomous coding agents are increasingly used in software development, shifting parts of the engineering process to AI assistance. While this automation brings clear benefits, it introduces challenges in governance, traceability, and control over agent behavior. Agent Context Files (ACFs) have emerged as a practical mechanism to guide agents through structured instructions, yet little is known about how these artifacts are maintained and how their evolution relates to code development. Objective. This paper plans to investigate the evolution of ACFs and their role in agent-driven development. Specifically, we (1) classify ACF changes through a taxonomy grounded in software maintenance theory, (2) analyze how different types of changes are associated with code quality outcomes, and (3) examine their temporal patterns across the development lifecycle. Method. We conduct a large-scale mining study combining repositories with ACFs and agent-generated commits. We reconstruct ACF evolution at the commit level, classify changes using a qualitative approach, and analyze their association with code quality metrics. Statistical analyses and hypotheses are used to evaluate differences across maintenance categories, to inform future design of ACFs for governing autonomous coding agents.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how Agent Context Files (ACFs) evolve in agent-driven software development, proposing a taxonomy of ACF changes, analyzing their association with code quality, and examining temporal patterns through a large-scale mining study.
AI.motivation: Autonomous coding agents are increasingly used but introduce challenges in governance, traceability, and control. ACFs guide agents via structured instructions, yet their maintenance and evolution relative to code development remain unexplored.
AI.method: Large-scale mining study combining repositories with ACFs and agent-generated commits; reconstruct ACF evolution at commit level, classify changes using qualitative approach, analyze association with code quality metrics, and use statistical hypotheses to evaluate differences across maintenance categories.
AI.result: A taxonomy of ACF changes grounded in software maintenance theory, insights into how different ACF change types associate with code quality outcomes, and temporal patterns of ACF evolution across the development lifecycle.
AI.conclusion: The findings inform future design of ACFs for governing autonomous coding agents by revealing maintenance patterns and their relationship to code quality.

title: Low-power analogue neural networks with trainable nonlinear connections for continuous control
comment: Preprint. Further verification of all simulations is ongoing. Any resulting corrections will be incorporated in a revised version
summary: Physical neural networks promise low-power machine learning by computing directly with analogue device physics, but most architectures force nonlinear device responses to act as scalar weights. Inspired by Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, we place trainable nonlinear functions on the connections, making each physical connection a learnable computational element. Realising these functions as analogue band-pass filters on field-programmable analogue arrays, we find that the benefit is task-dependent and follows from the smoothness of the physical basis: the networks represent smooth, continuously valued targets, including robotic kinematics, continuous control, and photovoltaic maximum-power-point tracking, with far fewer nodes and connections than multilayer perceptrons, but offer no parameter-efficiency advantage on classification-like decision boundaries. Trained networks transfer to hardware across approximately 35,000 connections with quantified fidelity, and a dedicated CMOS implementation is projected to operate at approximately 30 microwatts. A memristive realisation reproduces the same behaviour in simulation, indicating that the advantage comes from placing trainable nonlinearity on connections, rather than from a particular device.

AI.tldr: Physical neural networks with trainable nonlinear functions on connections (inspired by Kolmogorov-Arnold networks) implemented as analogue band-pass filters on field-programmable analogue arrays achieve parameter-efficient representation of smooth, continuous-valued targets (robotic kinematics, control, MPPT) using far fewer nodes than MLPs, but offer no advantage on classification tasks. Trained networks transfer to hardware across ~35,000 connections, with a projected 30 μW CMOS implementation.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of most physical neural network architectures that force nonlinear device responses to act as scalar weights, by placing trainable nonlinear functions on connections to make each physical connection a learnable computational element.
AI.method: Inspired by Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, trainable nonlinear functions are realized as analogue band-pass filters on field-programmable analogue arrays (FPAAs). The approach is evaluated on smooth continuous-valued targets (robotic kinematics, continuous control, MPPT) and discrete classification tasks. Hardware transfer is demonstrated across ~35,000 connections, and a dedicated CMOS and memristive realization are projected.
AI.result: The approach represents smooth continuous-valued targets with far fewer nodes and connections than MLPs, but offers no parameter-efficiency advantage on classification-like decision boundaries. Trained networks transfer to hardware with quantified fidelity, with projected ~30 μW power for CMOS and consistent behavior in memristive simulation.
AI.conclusion: The advantage of this approach comes from placing trainable nonlinearity on connections rather than from a particular device technology, enabling efficient physical neural networks for continuous-valued regression tasks.

title: Cross-View Variance Correlation in Path-Traced Stereo:A Hidden Shortcut in Synthetic Training Data
summary: Path-traced synthetic stereo data underlie a large fraction of modern disparity-estimation training pipelines. We report a previously unrecognised property of such data: while the Monte Carlo (MC) noise streams of the two cameras are statistically independent, the underlying \emph{variance fields} -- deterministic per-pixel functions of the rendering integrand -- are highly correlated once aligned by the ground-truth disparity warp. Across 20 scenes rendered with Mitsuba~3, the warped Pearson correlation reaches $ρ{=}0.754{\pm}0.016$ across 20 scenes at $\mathrm{SPP}{=}512$, and on a representative scene remains essentially invariant ($ρ{=}0.778{\pm}0.001$) over a $16\times$ range of samples per pixel. The effect is strongest in Lambertian regions ($ρ{\approx}0.78$) and substantially weaker in glass ($ρ{\approx}0.30$), as predicted by an integrand decomposition into view-independent and view-dependent components. A residual-shuffle intervention that breaks the cross-view alignment while preserving the clean image degrades the GT cost margin by $33\%$ on non-glass and the variance-based winner-take-all accuracy on glass by $4.3\times$, confirming the structure functions as a matching cue. This signal is unique to MC-rendered data and constitutes a candidate sim-to-real shortcut whose impact on trained networks remains to be quantified.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that variance fields in Monte Carlo-rendered synthetic stereo data are highly correlated across left and right views when aligned by ground-truth disparity, acting as an unintended matching cue that could create a sim-to-real shortcut for disparity estimation networks.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize a previously unrecognized property of path-traced synthetic stereo data—correlated variance fields—that may serve as a spurious signal for disparity estimation networks trained on such data.
AI.method: The authors render 20 scenes using Mitsuba 3, compute per-pixel variance fields from Monte Carlo noise, measure Pearson correlation between warped variance maps aligned by ground-truth disparity, analyze correlation dependence on SPP and material type (Lambertian vs. glass), and perform a residual-shuffle intervention to break cross-view variance correlation while preserving clean images.
AI.result: Warped Pearson correlation reaches ρ=0.754±0.016 across 20 scenes at SPP=512, remains essentially invariant (ρ=0.778±0.001) over a 16× SPP range, is strongest in Lambertian regions (ρ≈0.78) and weaker in glass (ρ≈0.30). Breaking the correlation degrades GT cost margin by 33% on non-glass and variance-based winner-take-all accuracy on glass by 4.3×.
AI.conclusion: Correlated variance fields in MC-rendered stereo data constitute a candidate sim-to-real shortcut whose impact on trained networks remains to be quantified.

title: Semantic Code Clone Detection: Are We There Yet?
summary:
Code clone detection has been extensively studied for decades, and recent approaches have begun reporting remarkably high performance for semantic (Type-4) clones on benchmark datasets. However, it remains unclear whether these results reflect a genuine ability to capture semantic equivalence between programs, or simply an ability to exploit dataset-specific patterns.
  In this paper, we present the first systematic empirical study investigating the generalizability of state-of-the-art (SOTA) semantic code clone detectors beyond benchmark evaluation settings. Inspired by the inherent inclusion relationship among clone types, we propose a clone operator framework consisting of eight transformation operators derived from Type-2 and Type-3 clone variations. Using these operators, we construct distribution-shifted yet semantically equivalent Type-4 clone instances and evaluate 11 representative detectors spanning token-based, tree-based, and graph-based paradigms on the real-world BigCloneBench dataset. Our results reveal substantial performance degradation across all evaluated approaches, despite their strong benchmark performance. Further analyses show that existing detectors heavily rely on shortcut learning based on lexical and structural cues rather than robust semantic understanding. Our findings suggest that current SOTA semantic code clone detectors exhibit limited generalizability in real-world scenarios, highlighting important avenues for future research.

AI.tldr: SOTA semantic code clone detectors show strong benchmark performance but suffer substantial degradation under distribution-shifted (Type-4) instances, revealing reliance on lexical/structural shortcuts rather than robust semantic understanding.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether high benchmark performance of semantic code clone detectors reflects genuine semantic understanding or exploitation of dataset-specific patterns.
AI.method: Proposed a clone operator framework with 8 transformation operators derived from Type-2 and Type-3 variations to construct distribution-shifted semantically equivalent Type-4 clone instances, then evaluated 11 detectors across token, tree, and graph paradigms on BigCloneBench.
AI.result: All 11 detectors exhibited substantial performance degradation under distribution-shifted instances despite strong benchmark scores; detectors rely on shortcut learning from lexical/structural cues.
AI.conclusion: Current SOTA semantic code clone detectors have limited generalizability in real-world scenarios, pointing to the need for more robust semantic understanding methods.

title: Synergizing Physically Constrained MCMC and Chemical-Informed Gaussian Processes for Reaction Network Discovery
summary: Extracting interpretable governing equations from sparse, noisy chemical time-series data remains difficult because discrete reaction topology and continuous kinetic parameters are tightly coupled. We present PC-MCMC-CIGP, a reproducible gray-box workflow that combines spike-and-slab topology sampling, hard conservation and thermodynamic screening, and a Chemical-Informed Gaussian Process (CIGP) residual model for parameter calibration and experimental design. The methodological contribution is not a new MCMC or GP family in isolation; rather, it is the integration of these components into a physically constrained workflow with explicit uncertainty-aware acquisition choices. On the H2 + Br2 benchmark, the constrained sampler distinguishes elementary radical pathways from deceptive phenomenological fits in our experiments. On styrene epoxidation, the CIGP optimization loop improves final yield by 12.5% over the reported GP-BO baseline. A new 10-seed acquisition study shows that EI, GWU, PC-EI, uncertainty sampling, discrepancy hunting, and random search have different trade-offs: PC-EI substantially reduces low-yield BO suggestions, while EI-style criteria give the strongest final-yield performance.

AI.tldr: PC-MCMC-CIGP is a gray-box workflow integrating spike-and-slab topology sampling, hard constraints, and Chemical-Informed Gaussian Processes for extracting interpretable governing equations from sparse, noisy chemical time-series data; it outperforms baselines on benchmark reactions (H2+Br2, styrene epoxidation) and analyzes acquisition function trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Extracting interpretable governing equations from sparse, noisy chemical time-series data remains difficult because discrete reaction topology and continuous kinetic parameters are tightly coupled.
AI.method: Integration of spike-and-slab topology sampling, hard conservation and thermodynamic screening, and a Chemical-Informed Gaussian Process (CIGP) residual model for parameter calibration and experimental design, with explicit uncertainty-aware acquisition choices.
AI.result: On H2+Br2, the constrained sampler distinguishes elementary radical pathways from deceptive phenomenological fits. On styrene epoxidation, CIGP optimization improves final yield by 12.5% over GP-BO baseline. PC-EI substantially reduces low-yield BO suggestions; EI-style criteria give strongest final-yield performance.
AI.conclusion: The integration of components into a physically constrained workflow with uncertainty-aware acquisition is the key contribution, not any single MCMC or GP method. Different acquisition functions (EI, GWU, PC-EI, etc.) exhibit distinct trade-offs between suggestion quality and final yield.

title: From Sparse and Imperfect 2D Anchors to Consistent 3D Gaussian Street Scenes: Support-Aware Appearance
summary: Image priors can synthesize target conditions for 3D Gaussian street scenes, but independently edited views do not define a coherent 3D target. Direct fitting can propagate view-specific noise, while existing pipelines do not jointly handle imperfect sparse anchors and standard-rasterizer deployment. To address this gap, teacher-relative appearance residual distillation is introduced for appearance baking. A structured space for frequency decomposition, confidence estimation, and primitive-level lifting is formed by residuals between teacher anchors and original renders. The direct optimization signal is supplied by renderer-space matching, while primitive assignment is regularized by support-aware Gaussian-space aggregation. Supported detail is admitted and unsupported noise is suppressed through confidence-gated coarse-to-fine optimization, after which all residuals are baked into fixed-geometry spherical-harmonic coefficients. The teacher and auxiliary training modules are discarded at inference. Evaluation across Waymo street assets, Tanks and Temples scenes, and multiple target conditions shows a favorable overall balance of target alignment, content preservation, artifact suppression, and cross-view consistency over editing-based baselines. Ablations confirm the effectiveness of the main components. Code will be released at https://github.com/Cagares/Baking-for-3D-Gaussian.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces teacher-relative appearance residual distillation for baking coherent appearance edits into 3D Gaussian street scenes, enabling consistent multi-view editing with suppressed artifacts while using only standard rasterizers at inference.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian editing methods either produce view-inconsistent results when edited independently, propagate view-specific noise through direct fitting, or fail to jointly handle imperfect sparse anchors and standard-rasterizer deployment.
AI.method: Teacher-relative appearance residual distillation: residuals between teacher anchors and original renders are decomposed via frequency decomposition, confidence estimation, and primitive-level lifting. Optimization uses renderer-space matching for direct signal, support-aware Gaussian-space aggregation for regularization, and confidence-gated coarse-to-fine optimization to admit supported detail while suppressing unsupported noise. Residuals are baked into fixed-geometry spherical-harmonic coefficients.
AI.result: Evaluation on Waymo street assets, Tanks and Temples, and multiple target conditions shows favorable balance of target alignment, content preservation, artifact suppression, and cross-view consistency over editing-based baselines. Ablations confirm effectiveness of main components.
AI.conclusion: The proposed residual distillation approach enables coherent appearance editing for 3D Gaussian splatting with standard-rasterizer deployment, achieving superior balance across key quality metrics compared to existing methods.

title: LibEvoBench: Probing Temporal Knowledge Stratification in Code Generation Models
comment: Accepted at the DL4Code workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Large software projects often depend on older versions of libraries, even as APIs continue to evolve across releases. This creates a challenge for LLMs: they must maintain knowledge of multiple API versions, not merely the latest or most common one. However, current LLMs are trained on temporally mixed corpora and lack explicit mechanisms for such version-specific reasoning, leading to anachronistic errors - calling APIs as they exist in a different library version. To systematically evaluate this phenomenon, we introduce LibEvoBench, a multi-task benchmark spanning multiple versions of widely used Python libraries, along with a new metric, the Software Evolution Understanding Score (SEUS), to measure models' consistency when working with evolving APIs. Our results show that state-of-the-art models are largely version-oblivious: performance degrades for evolving APIs, while for stable APIs it remains the same across versions. Moreover, simply specifying the target version provides no benefit, while relevant documentation significantly boosts models' accuracy. These findings highlight a systematic limitation of current training paradigms and motivate new approaches for temporally grounded knowledge in code generation.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with version-specific API reasoning across library releases, and LibEvoBench reveals that even specifying target versions doesn't help—only providing relevant documentation improves accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large software projects depend on older library versions, but LLMs are trained on temporally mixed corpora without mechanisms for version-specific reasoning, leading to anachronistic API errors. The authors aim to systematically evaluate this phenomenon.
AI.method: Introduced LibEvoBench, a multi-task benchmark spanning multiple versions of widely used Python libraries, and a new metric SEUS (Software Evolution Understanding Score) to measure consistency when working with evolving APIs.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models are largely version-oblivious: performance degrades for evolving APIs while stable APIs remain consistent. Specifying the target version alone provides no benefit, but relevant documentation significantly boosts accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Current training paradigms have a systematic limitation in handling temporally grounded knowledge, motivating new approaches for version-aware code generation.

title: Exploring Dualistic Meta-Learning to Enhance Domain Generalization in Open Set Scenarios
summary: Domain generalization learns from multiple source domains to generalize to unseen target domains. However, it often neglects the realistic case of label mismatch between source and target. Open set domain generalization is then proposed to recognize unseen classes in unseen domains. A simple approach trains one-vs-all classifiers to separate each class and detect outliers as unknown. Yet, the imbalance between few positive samples and many negative samples skews the decision boundary towards the positive ones, leading the model to over-reject out-of-distribution data, even from known classes in unseen domains. In this paper, we propose a novel meta-learning stategy called dualistic MEta-learning with joint DomaIn-Class matching (MEDIC), which considers implicit gradient matching towards inter-domain and inter-class task splits simultaneously to find optimal boundaries balanced for both domains and classes. Experimental results show that MEDIC not only outperforms prior methods in open set scenarios, but also maintains competitive close set generalization ability.

AI.tldr: MEDIC is a meta-learning strategy for open set domain generalization that handles both unseen classes and unseen domains by jointly matching domain and class gradients, outperforming prior methods in open set scenarios while maintaining competitive close set performance.
AI.motivation: Domain generalization methods often fail in realistic scenarios where there is label mismatch between source and target domains. Open set domain generalization addresses recognizing unseen classes in unseen domains. Simple one-vs-all classifiers suffer from severe imbalance between few positive and many negative samples, causing the model to over-reject out-of-distribution data including known classes in unseen domains.
AI.method: Proposes dualistic MEta-learning with joint DomaIn-Class matching (MEDIC), which performs implicit gradient matching towards both inter-domain and inter-class task splits simultaneously to learn optimal decision boundaries balanced for both domains and classes.
AI.result: MEDIC outperforms prior methods in open set scenarios and maintains competitive close set generalization ability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed meta-learning approach effectively addresses the label mismatch issue in domain generalization by jointly considering domain and class information, achieving superior open set performance without sacrificing close set capability.

title: Project-wise Comparison of Software Birthmarks Using Weighted Partial Similarity
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures, to be submitted to IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering
summary:
Software birthmarks provide a robust approach to detecting code plagiarism even under substantial modifications, while distinguishing independently developed software. Existing similarity measures are typically applied at the module level (e.g., source or class files). However, in practice, software reuse often occurs at the project level, where only a subset of modules may be reused. This setting introduces two key challenges: (1) partial reuse, where reused modules constitute only a small fraction of the project, and (2) incidental similarity from small modules, which can lead to false positives.
  In this paper, we establish a framework for project-wise birthmark comparison based on a symmetric aggregation of module-level similarities. On top of this framework, we propose two complementary mechanisms to address the above challenges. First, we introduce a weighting scheme that assigns higher importance to larger modules, reducing the influence of noisy matches from small modules. Second, we propose a partial similarity method that focuses on the top fraction of highly similar module pairs, enabling robust detection of partial reuse.
  We evaluate the proposed approach on 35 open-source Java projects across ten categories, where different versions of the same project are treated as reuse cases. The dataset and experimental artifacts are made publicly available to support reproducibility. Performance is assessed using two complementary properties of software birthmarks, resilience and credibility, combined via their harmonic mean. The results show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing approaches, achieving robust and stable detection of partial code reuse at the project level.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework for project-wise software birthmark comparison with weighting and partial similarity mechanisms to handle partial code reuse and incidental similarity, outperforming existing methods on 35 open-source Java projects.
AI.motivation: Existing similarity measures operate at the module level, but software reuse often occurs at the project level where only a subset of modules is reused. This introduces challenges: (1) partial reuse (reused modules are a small fraction of the project), and (2) incidental similarity from small modules causing false positives.
AI.method: The authors establish a symmetric aggregation framework for project-wise birthmark comparison. They propose: (1) a weighting scheme assigning higher importance to larger modules to reduce noise from small modules, and (2) a partial similarity method focusing on the top fraction of highly similar module pairs for robust partial reuse detection.
AI.result: Evaluated on 35 open-source Java projects across ten categories using resilience and credibility (harmonic mean). The proposed method consistently outperforms existing approaches, achieving robust and stable detection of partial code reuse at the project level.
AI.conclusion: The project-wise birthmark comparison framework with weighting and partial similarity mechanisms effectively addresses partial reuse and incidental similarity challenges, providing a robust solution for project-level code plagiarism detection.

title: One Ruler: A Same-Hands Re-Evaluation of Bivariate Causal Direction on Tuebingen, with a Parameter-Free Compression Baseline
comment: 15 pages, 1 table. Code, pre-registrations and per-pair outputs: https://github.com/WStienstra/one-ruler-cep
summary: Headline accuracies on the Tuebingen cause-effect pairs are routinely compared across papers even though each is measured under its authors' own protocol -- different pair subsets, weightings, model-selection, and decision rates. We argue this is the wrong comparison and run the right one: a same-hands re-evaluation in which every method is run by us on the identical 102 pairs, with one strict rule -- no tuning and a decision forced on every pair. As a clean reference point we introduce a deliberately minimal baseline: sorted-conditional compression, which feeds quantized, sorted, first-differenced data to an off-the-shelf compressor (bz2) and has zero fitted parameters. Under the common ruler the ranking differs sharply from the literature. Our baseline reaches 74.7% weighted accuracy (p = 3.7e-7); on the same 100 pairs that SLOPE is evaluated on it scores 76.0%, a 1.2-point gap below the authors' own forced-decision SLOPE (77.2%) that is well inside noise (McNemar p = 0.39). A faithful re-run of RECI lands at 70.7% -- inside the original authors' reported error bar, not the 77.5% often quoted (which we trace to a mis-copied cell). SLOPE's published 82.4% is a decided-subset figure: scoring the authors' own stored output only on the pairs its significance test chose to answer reproduces 81.7%. Under the common ruler the methods cluster in the low-to-mid 70s and the zero-parameter compressor ties the strongest of them. We document the mechanisms that inflate published figures (test-set model selection, significance-gated abstention) and contribute two further results: compression score magnitude is a model-free confounding flag (p = 2.8e-68), and a pre-registered falsification test fails in an instructive way that bounds the method's theoretical interpretation. Code, pre-registrations, and per-pair outputs are released.

AI.tldr: A same-hands re-evaluation of 12 causal inference methods on the Tuebingen cause-effect pairs (102 pairs) reveals that published rankings are distorted by inconsistent protocols, and a zero-parameter baseline (sorted-conditional compression with bz2) ties or beats most methods, reaching 74.7% weighted accuracy.
AI.motivation: Published headline accuracies on the Tuebingen benchmark are incomparable because each paper uses its own protocol (different subsets, weightings, model-selection, and decision rates), making literature rankings misleading.
AI.method: All methods were re-run by the authors on the identical 102 pairs with a strict no-tuning, forced-decision rule. A minimal baseline was introduced: sorted-conditional compression (quantized, sorted, first-differenced data → bz2 compressor, zero fitted parameters). Faithful re-runs of published methods (SLOPE, RECI, etc.) were performed.
AI.result: Under the common ruler, methods cluster in the low-to-mid 70% accuracy. Baseline reaches 74.7% (p=3.7e-7). On SLOPE's 100 pairs, baseline scores 76.0% vs SLOPE's 77.2% (McNemar p=0.39). RECI re-run lands at 70.7% (not the mis-copied 77.5%). SLOPE's 82.4% is a decided-subset figure; scoring only on answered pairs reproduces 81.7%.
AI.conclusion: Published rankings are inflated by test-set model selection and significance-gated abstention. A zero-parameter compressor ties the strongest methods. Compression score magnitude is a model-free confounding flag (p=2.8e-68). A pre-registered falsification test bounds the method's theoretical interpretation.

title: Unlocking Model Potentials Through Adaptive Multi-Agent Scaffolding for Efficient Issue Resolution
summary: Resolving issues with ambiguous and incomplete descriptions, particularly concerning complex bugs, requires a sophisticated, long-horizon workflow. Agents must navigate codebases to locate the root cause, reproduce the failure, implement a fix, and validate the resulting patch. Inefficient context management, thereby, can lead to rapid context degradation and context poisoning, preventing successful resolution. We propose icat-agent, a decentralized, multi-agent scaffolding that replaces shared context with synchronous, event-based message passing. Utilizing a rubric-based issue quality check, icat-agent strategically pivots its workflow: it initiates parallel patching and validation for well-defined issues, while deploying preliminary exploration for low-quality ones. A comprehensive evaluation of icat-agent on SWE-bench Verified and SWE-bench Pro demonstrates that it consistently outperforms prominent baselines across all difficulty levels, including SWE-agent, mini-SWE-agent, and Claude Code, while using the same underlying models, improving by 3.6-8.4% on SWE-bench Verified and 6.3-18.5% on SWE-bench Pro. icat-agent is also computationally efficient, reducing the average cost by $1.18 per instance compared with the multi-agent Claude Code baseline. Our findings reveal that a robust scaffold such as icat-agent unlocks substantial latent capability within a fixed model, with the same backbone resolving markedly more issues under icat-agent than under existing scaffolds. icat-agent +GPT-5.4-xhigh resolves 67.4% of SWE-bench Pro problems, outperforming the current best result on SWE-bench Pro (59.10%, mini-SWE-agent+GPT-5.4-xhigh) by 8.3 percentage points.

AI.tldr: icat-agent is a decentralized multi-agent scaffolding for software engineering that replaces shared context with event-based message passing, achieving state-of-the-art results on SWE-bench Verified (3.6-8.4% improvement) and SWE-bench Pro (6.3-18.5% improvement) while reducing computational costs.
AI.motivation: Existing agents suffer from inefficient context management (context degradation and poisoning) when resolving complex bugs with ambiguous/incomplete descriptions, preventing successful resolution of long-horizon software engineering tasks.
AI.method: icat-agent uses a decentralized multi-agent architecture with synchronous, event-based message passing (replacing shared context). It includes a rubric-based issue quality check to dynamically pivot: parallel patching+validation for well-defined issues, preliminary exploration for low-quality ones.
AI.result: Outperforms SWE-agent, mini-SWE-agent, and Claude Code on SWE-bench Verified (+3.6-8.4%) and SWE-bench Pro (+6.3-18.5%). Reduces average cost by $1.18/instance vs Claude Code. icat-agent+GPT-5.4-xhigh achieves 67.4% on SWE-bench Pro, surpassing the previous best 59.10% by 8.3 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: A robust scaffold (icat-agent) unlocks substantial latent capability within a fixed model, with the same backbone resolving markedly more issues under icat-agent than existing scaffolds.

title: Deciphering Fingerprints of 3D Molecular Surfaces for Accurate Epitope Prediction
summary: Molecular surfaces encode the geometric and physicochemical patterns that determine antibody-antigen recognition, central to epitope prediction. However, existing methods rely on sequences or backbone structures and struggle to capture discontinuous, surface-driven epitopes. This study presents SurfBind, a surface-centric learning framework for epitope prediction that operates directly on molecular surface representations. SurfBind integrates geometric and physicochemical cues through a Transformer-based architecture with patch-level surface modeling, binder-aware cross-attention, and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine prediction paradigm. Experiments on challenging epitope identification benchmarks, including SAbDab and DB5.5, demonstrate that SurfBind achieves state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization across unseen antibodies and conformational states, highlighting the value of interaction-aware surface modeling for understanding the crucial mechanisms of protein-protein interactions.

AI.tldr: SurfBind is a surface-centric learning framework that directly operates on molecular surface representations for epitope prediction, achieving state-of-the-art performance by integrating geometric and physicochemical cues through Transformer-based architecture with patch-level surface modeling and binder-aware cross-attention.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on sequences or backbone structures and struggle to capture discontinuous, surface-driven epitopes, motivating the need for a surface-centric approach that directly models molecular surfaces encoding geometric and physicochemical patterns for antibody-antigen recognition.
AI.method: SurfBind uses a Transformer-based architecture with patch-level surface modeling, binder-aware cross-attention, and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine prediction paradigm, operating directly on molecular surface representations that integrate geometric and physicochemical cues.
AI.result: SurfBind achieves state-of-the-art performance on SAbDab and DB5.5 benchmarks with strong generalization across unseen antibodies and conformational states.
AI.conclusion: Interaction-aware surface modeling is valuable for understanding the crucial mechanisms of protein-protein interactions and epitope prediction.

title: The impact of artificial intelligence on enterprise software user roles
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly reshaping the nature of work in software development, transforming user roles, workflows, and collaboration patterns across enterprise platforms. This qualitative study investigates how AI alters professional responsibilities within the context of SAP's Business Technology Platform (BTP), combining expert interviews (n=20) and a participatory workshop (n=24). The results reveal substantial shifts in day-to-day tasks and roles in the development domain, characterized by increasing automation of operational tasks, expanding human-AI collaboration, and growing reliance on agentic AI systems. The study further identifies significant implications for existing user-role frameworks, such as the BTP User Type Matrix, which requires adaptation as the workforce is undergoing significant role specific changes. Collectively, these findings highlight a workforce landscape in transition and underscore the need for revised role taxonomies, new governance and oversight functions, and updated design approaches for AI-native enterprise software systems.

AI.tldr: This qualitative study (n=44) investigates how AI reshapes professional roles and workflows in software development within SAP's Business Technology Platform, finding substantial shifts toward automation, human-AI collaboration, and agentic AI systems, necessitating updated role taxonomies, governance functions, and design approaches for AI-native enterprise software.
AI.motivation: To understand how AI is transforming professional roles, tasks, and collaboration patterns in enterprise software development, specifically within SAP's Business Technology Platform, as existing user-role frameworks may be inadequate for the evolving AI-driven workforce.
AI.method: Qualitative study combining expert interviews (n=20) and a participatory workshop (n=24), analyzed within the context of SAP's Business Technology Platform.
AI.result: Identified three major shifts: increasing automation of operational tasks, expanding human-AI collaboration, and growing reliance on agentic AI systems. Found that existing frameworks (e.g., BTP User Type Matrix) require adaptation due to significant role-specific changes in the workforce.
AI.conclusion: The workforce is in transition, requiring revised role taxonomies, new governance and oversight functions, and updated design approaches for AI-native enterprise software systems.

title: Reconstructing GRACE Terrestrial Water Storage with Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks: An Application to South America
summary: Terrestrial water storage (TWS) integrates snow, soil moisture, surface water, and groundwater and is a key indicator of how climate variability and human activity reshape the global water cycle. The GRACE and GRACE-FO satellite missions provide the only direct, globally consistent observations of TWS change, but their record only begins in 2002 which is too short for many climate-scale analyses. We present a deep learning application that reconstructs monthly GRACE-like TWS anomalies (TWSA) back to 1940 by learning the relationship between daily ERA5 meteorological forcing (precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff) and monthly GRACE observations. In contrast to prior reconstruction approaches based on grid-cell-wise regression, CNNs, or LSTMs, we adapt a multi-variate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) architecture, which was originally developed for mobility and traffic forecasting on urban sensor networks to this satellite-geodesy task. Spatial dependencies are encoded in a static, interpretable hybrid adjacency matrix that combines geodesic proximity with lagged correlations of climatic time series, capturing both local hydrological coupling and large-scale teleconnections. The reconstruction achieves a grid-cell Pearson correlation of 0.69, a basin-mean correlation of 0.94, and a near-zero bias, and it reproduces the spatial fingerprints of the 2015/16 El Niño and 2020/21 La Niña events. A systematic comparison with established reconstruction approaches (GTWS-MLrec, RM-REC, GRAiCE) shows that the graph-based model is statistically competitive at basin scale, reaching a correlation within 0.025 of the best baseline while using only roughly half to a tenth of the predictors the other models require and revealing characteristic weaknesses in arid regions in all models. The complete implementation is publicly available at github.com/hcu-cml/MTGNN-TWS-Reconstruction-GRACE

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deep learning approach using a multivariate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) to reconstruct GRACE-like terrestrial water storage anomalies back to 1940 by learning from ERA5 meteorological forcing and GRACE observations, achieving strong performance with fewer predictors than prior methods.
AI.motivation: The GRACE satellite record of terrestrial water storage anomalies only begins in 2002, which is too short for many climate-scale analyses. A longer-term reconstruction is needed to understand how climate variability and human activity have reshaped the global water cycle over a multi-decadal timeframe.
AI.method: The authors adapt a multivariate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) originally designed for mobility/traffic forecasting. They use daily ERA5 meteorological forcings (precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff) as inputs to reconstruct monthly GRACE-like TWSA.
