Spatial dependencies are encoded via a static, interpretable hybrid adjacency matrix combining geodesic proximity with lagged correlations of climatic time series, capturing both local hydrological coupling and large-scale teleconnections.
AI.result: The reconstruction achieves grid-cell Pearson correlation of 0.69, basin-mean correlation of 0.94, and near-zero bias. It reproduces spatial fingerprints of the 2015/16 El Niño and 2020/21 La Niña events. The graph-based model is statistically competitive at basin scale (within 0.025 correlation of best baseline) while using only about half to a tenth of the predictors required by other models.
AI.conclusion: The MTGNN-based approach provides a statistically competitive, parsimonious reconstruction of TWS anomalies back to 1940, revealing characteristic weaknesses in arid regions across all compared models. The implementation is publicly available.

title: Evaluating LLMs on Real-World Software Performance Optimization
summary: Software performance optimization is a notoriously complex and manual task. Despite the growing use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for code refinement, we still lack benchmarks that capture how optimization actually happens in real-world codebases. Existing frameworks often oversimplify the problem by focusing on isolated functions or a single performance metric, missing the critical trade-offs between execution time and memory footprint, the inherent noise of the measurement environment, and the variability introduced by different input data and execution conditions. We address this by introducing SWE-Pro, a repository-level benchmark derived from 102 expert-written optimizations from open-source projects. Unlike previous benchmarks, SWE-Pro pairs each task with parameterized tests to evaluate runtime, peak memory, and Time-Weighted Memory Usage (TWMU) across varying input data and execution conditions under noise-aware measurement conditions. Our evaluation shows that current LLMs struggle significantly: runtime gains are negligible, and memory optimizations are nearly non-existent. This stands in sharp contrast to expert implementations, which achieve an aggregate speedup of 15.5x and peak memory reduction of 171.3x over benchmark tasks. Expert-written improvements are observed in 91.2% of tasks for runtime and 65.7% for peak memory. Our findings expose a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the demands of expert-level engineering.

AI.tldr: SWE-Pro is a repository-level benchmark of 102 expert-written software optimizations that evaluates LLMs on runtime and memory trade-offs under noisy, real-world conditions, revealing LLMs achieve negligible improvements compared to expert speedups of 15.5x and memory reductions of 171.3x.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks oversimplify software optimization by focusing on isolated functions or single metrics, ignoring real-world complexities like execution time vs. memory trade-offs, measurement noise, and input variability. There is a need for a benchmark that captures how optimization actually happens in real-world codebases.
AI.method: SWE-Pro is a repository-level benchmark derived from 102 expert-written optimizations from open-source projects. Each task includes parameterized tests to evaluate runtime, peak memory, and Time-Weighted Memory Usage (TWMU) across varying input data and execution conditions under noise-aware measurement.
AI.result: Current LLMs show negligible runtime gains and nearly non-existent memory optimizations. Experts achieve 15.5x aggregate speedup and 171.3x peak memory reduction, with improvements in 91.2% of tasks for runtime and 65.7% for peak memory.
AI.conclusion: There is a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the demands of expert-level software optimization, highlighting the need for further research in LLM-based code optimization.

title: The Degeneracy Distillery
comment: 30 pages, 10 figures. Supporting code found at https://github.com/tlmakinen/degeneracy_distillery
summary: When two or more parameters or labels produce similar data, they are degenerate, or hard to distinguish. Degeneracies render both label prediction and inverse problems difficult, since both machine learning algorithms and probabilistic samplers rely on the distinguishability of data and its gradients with respect to parameters. However, identifying degeneracies in physical models or real-world datasets can be elucidating about the choice of model or the underlying process that produces the data. We present the degeneracy distillery, a method that (1) detects and (2) resolves degenerate parameter combinations (a) automatically and (b) symbolically, from parameter-data (or parameter-simulation) pairs alone, through estimation and flattening of the Fisher information matrix. By exploring the information geometry of the likelihood, we characterize degeneracies as an intrinsic property of the physical model, requiring no realised data observation. We demonstrate our approach on a range of synthetic and real-world problems, discovering symbolic coordinate transformations that identify the combinations of parameters of a model which yield independent effects on the data. The resulting coordinates flatten the Fisher information in expectation globally, in contrast to posterior-based methods that flatten only at a single point, and substantially reduce the simulation budget required for downstream neural posterior estimation. In test cases we require up to $10\times$ fewer simulations for posterior estimation at matched validation calibration whilst simultaneously gaining physical insight on the system.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the degeneracy distillery, a method that automatically detects and resolves degenerate parameter combinations in physical models by estimating and flattening the Fisher information matrix. This approach identifies symbolic coordinate transformations that yield independent effects on data, reduces simulation budgets for neural posterior estimation by up to 10x, and provides physical insight into the system.
AI.motivation: Degeneracies (where different parameter/label combinations produce similar data) hinder label prediction, inverse problems, machine learning, and probabilistic sampling. Identifying these degeneracies is crucial for understanding model selection and underlying data-generating processes.
AI.method: The degeneracy distillery method (1) detects and (2) resolves degenerate parameter combinations automatically and symbolically from parameter-data pairs by estimating and flattening the Fisher information matrix. It explores the information geometry of the likelihood to characterize degeneracies as intrinsic properties of the physical model, requiring no observed data.
AI.result: The method discovers symbolic coordinate transformations identifying parameter combinations with independent effects on data. It globally flattens the Fisher information in expectation (unlike posterior-based methods that flatten only at a single point), and reduces simulation budgets for neural posterior estimation by up to 10x at matched validation calibration.
AI.conclusion: The degeneracy distillery effectively identifies and resolves degeneracies in physical models, provides physical insight, and substantially improves efficiency of downstream neural posterior estimation.

title: On the Viability of Requirements Generation From Code: An Experience Report
comment: Accepted for publication at the 13th International Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Requirements Engineering (AIRE'26)
summary: Empirical research in Requirements Engineering is hampered by a lack of adequate datasets that pair source code with corresponding requirements. A tempting route to addressing this lack is the use of Large Language Models to synthesize requirements from existing code bases. We investigate this question by evaluating an LLM-based and RAG-supported agentic approach that generates requirements from source code, verifies their implementation status relying on a human-in-the-loop, and synthetically introduces requirements smells and non-implemented requirements. Our goal was to create datasets that mimic reality and foster empirical RE research. However, during the study, various problems arose, leading to this experience report. Contrary to our initial hypotheses, LLMs were unable to (i) generate non-implemented requirements reliably, (ii) generate high quality requirements, and (iii) reliably introduce synthetic requirements smells. Furthermore, neither an LLM nor a single human-in-the-loop suffices to detect requirements smells reliably. These findings suggest that the generation of code-to-requirements datasets using LLMs is not yet viable and requires human supervision, especially for quality assurance. We critically reflect on our lessons learned and draw relevant conclusions for both researchers and practitioners.

AI.tldr: LLMs are not yet viable for generating reliable code-to-requirements datasets due to failures in producing non-implemented requirements, high-quality requirements, and synthetic smells, and neither LLMs nor a single human-in-the-loop can reliably detect requirements smells.
AI.motivation: Empirical research in Requirements Engineering suffers from a lack of adequate datasets pairing source code with corresponding requirements. The authors explored using LLMs to synthesize requirements from existing code bases to address this gap.
AI.method: The authors developed an LLM-based and RAG-supported agentic approach that generates requirements from source code, verifies implementation status with a human-in-the-loop, and synthetically introduces requirements smells and non-implemented requirements.
AI.result: LLMs failed to: (i) reliably generate non-implemented requirements, (ii) generate high-quality requirements, and (iii) reliably introduce synthetic requirements smells. Additionally, neither an LLM nor a single human-in-the-loop suffices to reliably detect requirements smells.
AI.conclusion: The generation of code-to-requirements datasets using LLMs is not yet viable and requires human supervision, particularly for quality assurance. The authors provide lessons learned and conclusions for both researchers and practitioners.

title: Machine Learning Modeling for Real-Time Melt Pool Monitoring in Laser Powder Bed Fusion Additive Manufacturing: A Hybrid Approach
summary: This work investigates the implementation of artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) for real-time monitoring in laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) additive manufacturing. We developed a binary image classification framework for distinguishing normal and abnormal melt pool images using a balanced dataset of 1,200 images collected from Nickel superalloy 625 on the NIST AMMT platform. The study evaluates accuracy and inference time based on control requirements and hardware limitations of open-architecture LPBF machines. We benchmark three transfer learning architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV2) against two Random Forest approaches: one trained on EfficientNetB0 feature embeddings (hybrid) and one trained on raw pixel features (baseline). Images are stratified into 80/20 train-test splits, with a further 90/10 validation split on the training set, and undergo standardized resizing, normalization, and label-preserving data augmentation to emulate realistic process variability. Each model is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), along with training time, inference latency, and CPU & GPU usage to capture deployability constraints relevant to factory-floor monitoring. The hybrid EfficientNetB0-plus-Random Forest approach achieves the best performance on the held-out test set, with an F1 score of 0.9451, accuracy of 0.9458, and AUC of 0.9904, while maintaining sub-millisecond per-image inference (1.15 ms). In contrast, purely deep learning models exhibit significantly higher inference times with lower accuracy. These results demonstrate that combining pre-trained convolutional features with classical ensemble methods provides a robust, computationally efficient route to real-time melt pool anomaly detection in data-limited additive manufacturing environments.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a binary image classification framework for real-time melt pool anomaly detection in LPBF additive manufacturing, comparing deep learning and hybrid models. The hybrid EfficientNetB0-plus-Random Forest approach achieves the best performance (F1: 0.9451, accuracy: 0.9458, AUC: 0.9904) with sub-millisecond inference (1.15 ms), demonstrating that combining pre-trained convolutional features with classical ensemble methods offers a robust, computationally efficient solution for data-limited environments.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time monitoring in laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) additive manufacturing by developing a computationally efficient and accurate AI/ML framework for distinguishing normal and abnormal melt pool images, while addressing the constraints of open-architecture LPBF machines and factory-floor deployability.
AI.method: A binary image classification framework was developed using a balanced dataset of 1,200 melt pool images from Nickel superalloy 625 collected on the NIST AMMT platform. Three transfer learning architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2) were benchmarked against two Random Forest approaches: one trained on EfficientNetB0 feature embeddings (hybrid) and one trained on raw pixel features (baseline). Images underwent 80/20 train-test split with 90/10 validation split, standardized resizing, normalization, and label-preserving data augmentation. Models were evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC, training time, inference latency, and CPU/GPU usage.
AI.result: The hybrid EfficientNetB0-plus-Random Forest approach achieved the best performance on the held-out test set with F1 score of 0.9451, accuracy of 0.9458, and AUC of 0.9904, while maintaining sub-millisecond per-image inference (1.15 ms). Purely deep learning models exhibited significantly higher inference times with lower accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Combining pre-trained convolutional features with classical ensemble methods provides a robust, computationally efficient route to real-time melt pool anomaly detection in data-limited additive manufacturing environments, suitable for factory-floor deployment.

title: IntentTester: Intent-Driven Multi-agent Framework for Cross-Library Test Migration
summary: Unit tests capture both functional checks and domain-specific knowledge, but this knowledge remains locked within individual projects and is rarely reused across libraries with overlapping functionality. Existing migration techniques based on structural code mappings (e.g., API signatures) often break down under divergent designs or cross-language settings, resulting in non-executable migrated tests. In this paper, we present IntentTester, a multi-agent framework for intent-driven test reuse. Instead of translating raw code, IntentTester abstracts tests into a language-agnostic Test Description Language (TDL), aligns them with semantically related entities and dependencies in a repository graph, and synthesizes executable tests through LLM-guided reasoning and iterative validation. This design enables cross-library and cross-language migration without manual intervention, producing migrated tests that existing structure-mapping approaches cannot achieve. We evaluate IntentTester on nine open-source projects across three domains (JSON, HTML, and Time) and two languages (Java and Python). IntentTester generates 2,776 syntactically correct tests with 85\% correctness; in comparison, the two baselines achieve 51\% and 43\%. Among them, 2,410 tests executed successfully, yielding a 74\% effectiveness rate. Beyond higher success rates, IntentTester also surfaced previously unknown defects including stack overflows, null dereferences, and parsing inconsistencies, several of which have been acknowledged or patched by maintainers. Our results show that intent-driven migration shifts the focus from code mappings to semantic alignment, allowing practical cross-library and cross-language test reuse while improving test quality and exposing implementation flaws.

AI.tldr: IntentTester is a multi-agent framework for intent-driven test reuse that abstracts tests into a language-agnostic Test Description Language (TDL), enabling cross-library and cross-language test migration without manual intervention. It achieves 85% correctness (2,776 tests) across nine open-source projects, significantly outperforming baselines (51% and 43%), and uncovered previously unknown defects including stack overflows and null dereferences.
AI.motivation: Unit tests contain valuable domain-specific knowledge that is locked within individual projects and rarely reused across libraries with overlapping functionality. Existing migration techniques based on structural code mappings (e.g., API signatures) often fail under divergent designs or cross-language settings, producing non-executable migrated tests.
AI.method: IntentTester abstracts tests into a language-agnostic Test Description Language (TDL), aligns them with semantically related entities and dependencies in a repository graph, and synthesizes executable tests through LLM-guided reasoning and iterative validation.
AI.result: Evaluated on nine open-source projects across three domains (JSON, HTML, Time) and two languages (Java, Python). IntentTester generated 2,776 syntactically correct tests with 85% correctness (baselines: 51% and 43%). 2,410 tests executed successfully (74% effectiveness rate). Surfaces previously unknown defects including stack overflows, null dereferences, and parsing inconsistencies, several acknowledged or patched by maintainers.
AI.conclusion: Intent-driven migration shifts focus from code mappings to semantic alignment, enabling practical cross-library and cross-language test reuse while improving test quality and exposing implementation flaws.

title: Sesame: Structure-Aware Molecular Generation via Spatial Density-Map Conditioning
summary: Generative molecular models for drug design are a promising direction with much active research. In the next phase of computational drug design, such models will need to understand small molecule structure and protein-ligand interactions, and they will need to possess the machinery to generate molecules de novo. Incorporating each feature poses a critical challenge. Equally important, yet often treated as secondary, is the ability to grow a molecule from a partial starting point -- a scaffold or fragment supplied by a chemist -- which is the central operation of lead optimization. We present Sesame (Spatial Evoformer for a Structure-Aware Molecular Engine), a diffusion-based molecular generation model that leverages a novel spatial pairformer module to condition on partial molecular structure and the surrounding protein pocket, both expressed as continuous spatial density maps. This single conditioning mechanism supports both de novo generation and fragment-conditioned lead optimization, letting a medicinal chemist prune a hit to a scaffold and have Sesame grow it in productive ways. In addition to this module, we also introduce a diffusion framework for joint denoising of atom types, bond types, and positions, along with a trajectory finetuning scheme that trains on the model's own sampling rollouts to improve generation quality. Sesame is trained on a large corpus of ligand-only and protein-ligand datasets.

AI.tldr: Sesame is a diffusion-based molecular generation model that uses a spatial pairformer module to condition on both partial molecular structures (scaffolds/fragments) and protein pockets, enabling de novo drug design and lead optimization with a single framework.
AI.motivation: Existing generative molecular models lack the ability to effectively incorporate fragment-conditioned growth (lead optimization) alongside de novo generation, and often treat the former as secondary despite its central role in drug design.
AI.method: Sesame uses a spatial pairformer module that conditions on partial molecular structures and surrounding protein pockets via continuous spatial density maps, a joint diffusion framework for denoising atom types, bond types, and positions, and a trajectory finetuning scheme trained on its own sampling rollouts.
AI.result: The single conditioning mechanism supports both de novo generation and fragment-conditioned lead optimization, enabling medicinal chemists to prune hits to scaffolds and grow them productively.
AI.conclusion: Sesame unifies de novo generation and scaffold-based lead optimization through a structure-aware diffusion framework trained on ligand-only and protein-ligand datasets, with trajectory finetuning improving generation quality.

title: CodeChat-Eval: Evaluating Large Language Models in Multi-Turn Code Refinement Dialogues
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used in software engineering to generate and refine code. In practice, developers often continue from an initial code generation request with follow-up refinement instructions, such as requests to improve style, restructure implementation, or change the execution strategy while preserving the intended behaviour. However, existing benchmarks generally omit this multi-turn code refinement dialogue setting and therefore cannot evaluate whether LLMs maintain functional correctness, i.e., whether the refined code still passes the test suite for the original task. To address this limitation, we introduce CodeChat-Eval, an evaluation framework that constructs evaluation sessions from multi-turn code refinement dialogues using a dynamic instruction selection algorithm. Our empirical study on open-weight and proprietary LLMs observes a statistically significant decrease ranging from 19.2% (GPT-5 Nano) to 69.2% (Llama 3.1 8B) in functional correctness over multi-turn refinement. The largest correctness drops are associated with logic-level refinements and additive change requests. These findings indicate that LLMs struggle to maintain functional correctness during multi-turn code refinement dialogues, and highlight the need for benchmarks that evaluate functionality-preserving refinement beyond single-turn generation.

AI.tldr: CodeChat-Eval evaluates LLMs on multi-turn code refinement dialogues and finds statistically significant functional correctness drops (19.2%–69.2%) across models, especially for logic-level and additive changes.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks only evaluate single-turn code generation and cannot assess whether LLMs maintain functional correctness when developers issue follow-up refinement instructions (e.g., improve style, restructure, change execution strategy) in multi-turn dialogues.
AI.method: CodeChat-Eval constructs evaluation sessions from multi-turn code refinement dialogues using a dynamic instruction selection algorithm. Empirical study on open-weight and proprietary LLMs.
AI.result: Statistically significant decrease in functional correctness ranging from 19.2% (GPT-5 Nano) to 69.2% (Llama 3.1 8B) over multi-turn refinement. Largest drops occur for logic-level refinements and additive change requests.
AI.conclusion: LLMs struggle to maintain functional correctness during multi-turn code refinement dialogues, highlighting the need for benchmarks that evaluate functionality-preserving refinement beyond single-turn generation.

title: Are Safety Guarantees in Neural Networks Safe? How to Compute Trustworthy Robustness Certifications
summary: A primary challenge in AI safety is the existence of adversarial examples -- slightly distorted inputs that cause a neural network (NN) to misclassify. To mitigate this problem, recent research focuses on the computation of robustness certifications, which, for a given input, determine the largest distortion the input may receive without breaking the network's prediction. Robustness certifications can be interpreted as an axis-aligned hyper-rectangle (multi-dimensional intervals). Most existing approaches focus on maximizing the certification's volume, but recent intractability results prohibit the computation of volume-optimal certifications in reasonable time. We introduce the apothem measure and show how to compute apothem-optimal certifications in a linear number of calls to a NN verifier (oracle) w.r.t. the input domain's diameter. Moreover, we prove that we cannot have a volume-optimal, oracle-based algorithm, even if we discard the oracle costs. Also, we introduce dual certifications -- an interval including all instances of a class -- thus providing apothem-minimum upper bounds to a robustness certification. Further, we present the ParallelepipedoNN system, which we evaluate on the standard MNIST and Fashion MNIST benchmarks. A preliminary comparison with existing work on the same datasets reveals at least two-fold improvement w.r.t. the minimum edge length.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the apothem measure for computing robustness certifications of neural networks, showing apothem-optimal certifications can be computed in linear oracle calls, proves volume-optimal oracle-based algorithms are impossible, introduces dual certifications for upper bounds, and presents the ParallelepipedoNN system achieving at least 2x improvement in minimum edge length on MNIST benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Robustness certifications (axis-aligned hyper-rectangles) are crucial for AI safety against adversarial examples, but volume-optimal certification computation is intractable in reasonable time.
AI.method: Introduces the apothem measure as an alternative objective; computes apothem-optimal certifications with linear oracle calls (w.r.t. input domain diameter); proves impossibility of volume-optimal oracle-based algorithms; introduces dual certifications for apothem-minimum upper bounds.
AI.result: ParallelepipedoNN system evaluated on MNIST and Fashion MNIST achieves at least two-fold improvement in minimum edge length compared to existing work.
AI.conclusion: Apothem-optimal certifications are tractable (linear oracle calls) while volume-optimal ones are not, and the proposed approach yields significantly better minimum edge length in practice.

authors: Paulo Anselmo da Mota Silveira Neto
title: Automated Detection of Configuration-Specific Security Vulnerabilities via Patch Analysis
summary: We study how security patches in highly configurable C/C++ systems map onto the space of compile-time variants. We formalize the Vulnerability Impact Condition (VIC) - a Boolean predicate over configuration options that denotes all variants that contained the original flaw - and introduce PatchLens, a purely static technique that recovers VICs by aligning AST-level patch hunks with source-level presence conditions and resolving file inclusion via lightweight build system analysis. Evaluating PatchLens on 1,192 Linux kernel, 289 FFmpeg, and 100 PHP patches, we compute precise, human-readable VICs without the need to compile any system variant. The resulting predicates are compact (avg. 1.84 variables for Linux, 3.23 for FFmpeg, 1.04 for PHP) and show that only a small fraction of vulnerabilities are system-wide, which carry higher CVSS scores; meanwhile, CVE texts almost never encode the required options ($\approx$ 1% average recall), motivating automated enrichment of CVE descriptions with VICs. PatchLens and the accompanying dataset enable immediate applications in CI (variant-aware triage and test selection), targeted sampling and fuzzing, and feature risk scoring, offering a scalable, explainable path to vulnerability assessment in highly configurable software.

AI.tldr: PatchLens is a static technique that recovers Vulnerability Impact Conditions (VICs) to map security patches in configurable C/C++ systems onto compile-time variants, evaluated on 1,192 Linux, 289 FFmpeg, and 100 PHP patches, finding VICs are compact (avg. 1.04–3.23 variables) and most vulnerabilities are not system-wide.
AI.motivation: Security patches in highly configurable C/C++ systems affect only specific compile-time variants, but existing approaches lack a way to precisely and automatically identify which configuration option combinations contain the original vulnerability, hampering variant-aware triage, testing, and risk assessment.
AI.method: PatchLens formalizes Vulnerability Impact Conditions (VICs) as Boolean predicates over configuration options, and recovers them via purely static analysis by aligning AST-level patch hunks with source-level presence conditions and resolving file inclusion through lightweight build system analysis, without compiling any variant.
AI.result: On 1,192 Linux, 289 FFmpeg, and 100 PHP patches, PatchLens produces precise, human-readable VICs that are compact (avg. 1.84, 3.23, 1.04 variables respectively); only a small fraction of vulnerabilities are system-wide (those carrying higher CVSS scores); CVE texts encode required options at only ~1% average recall.
AI.conclusion: PatchLens enables variant-aware CI triage, targeted sampling/fuzzing, and feature risk scoring, offering a scalable, explainable path to vulnerability assessment in configurable software, and motivates automated enrichment of CVE descriptions with VICs.

title: Exact Schur-Sylvester Dimensionality Reductions for Non-Smooth Stochastic Complexity and Manifold Sampling
summary: The exact computation of the Normalized Maximum Likelihood (NML) codelength for regular non-smooth estimators (e.g., Lasso) has been historically limited by the cubic scaling walls of manifold-constrained projection and volume integration. At each step of the geometric Propose-and-Project Metropolis--Hastings (PPMH) sampler, evaluating the projection operator requires inverting an $(N+k) \times (N+k)$ generalized KKT matrix, while calculating the volume factor requires the determinant of an $(N-k) \times (N-k)$ Gram matrix. This paper presents an exact, mathematically equivalent formulation that bypasses both bottlenecks by utilizing the block Schur complement and Sylvester's determinant identity. We prove that the computational complexity of both operations collapses from $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(k^3 + N^2 k)$ per step. We generalize this reduction to Sparse Support Vector Machines (SVMs), Elastic Net, and Group Lasso. Finally, we provide a rigorous numerical stability analysis and evaluate the sampler's efficiency using the Effective Sample Size (ESS) per second. Our empirical benchmarks on high-dimensional datasets confirm a constant speedup exceeding $14{,}100\times$ while maintaining double-precision numerical equivalence, rendering exact non-smooth NML estimation highly tractable for large-scale statistical inference.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an exact reformulation of the PPMH sampler for NML codelength computation that reduces per-step complexity from O(N³) to O(k³ + N²k) by using block Schur complement and Sylvester's determinant identity, achieving >14,100× speedup for non-smooth estimators like Lasso, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso.
AI.motivation: Exact NML codelength computation for regular non-smooth estimators (e.g., Lasso) has been computationally prohibitive due to cubic-scaling bottlenecks in manifold-constrained projection (requiring inversion of an (N+k)×(N+k) KKT matrix) and volume integration (requiring determinant of an (N−k)×(N−k) Gram matrix) at each PPMH sampling step.
AI.method: The authors derive an exact, mathematically equivalent formulation that replaces the costly KKT matrix inversion and Gram determinant computation using block Schur complement and Sylvester's determinant identity. This reduction is generalized to Sparse SVMs, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso, accompanied by a numerical stability analysis.
AI.result: Computational complexity collapses from O(N³) to O(k³ + N²k) per PPMH step. Empirical benchmarks on high-dimensional datasets demonstrate a constant speedup exceeding 14,100× while maintaining double-precision numerical equivalence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reformulation renders exact non-smooth NML estimation highly tractable for large-scale statistical inference, removing the historical cubic scaling barrier that limited practical applicability.

title: LLM-Based Discovery of Latent Requirements from Stakeholder Conversations: Preliminary Results from Industry
comment: This paper has been accepted at the 13th International Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Requirements Engineering (AIRE 2026), co-located with RE 2026
summary: Stakeholder interviews are an important source of information for requirements elicitation, yet many relevant requirements remain implicit in such conversations. Stakeholders frequently describe workflows, challenges, and operational practices without explicitly articulating the software capabilities that could address them. Recent work has considered the use of LLMs to analyze conversational data and extract requirements from stakeholder interviews. Existing approaches, however, primarily focus on identifying explicitly stated requirements, leaving implicit opportunities largely unexplored. In this paper, we present LENS (LLM-Enabled Needs Discovery from Stakeholder Interviews), an approach that analyzes stakeholder interview transcripts to both extract explicit requirements and infer additional latent requirements. LENS performs this inference by reasoning over stakeholder statements together with contextual information about organizational tools and infrastructure. Both extracted and inferred requirements are represented as user stories and linked to transcript excerpts to ensure traceability. We conduct a preliminary evaluation of LENS using twelve stakeholder interview transcripts collected in an industrial setting involving cybersecurity operations. We show that LENS achieves an average F1-score of 84.4% for extracting explicit requirements, while, on average, 75% of the latent requirements identified by LENS were perceived as providing useful automation or time-saving potential by domain experts.

AI.tldr: LENS is an LLM-based approach that analyzes stakeholder interview transcripts to both extract explicit requirements and infer latent (implicit) requirements by reasoning over statements and contextual organizational information, achieving 84.4% F1 for extraction and 75% of latent requirements deemed useful by domain experts.
AI.motivation: Stakeholder interviews contain rich information for requirements elicitation, but many relevant requirements remain implicit. Existing LLM-based approaches focus on identifying explicitly stated requirements, leaving implicit opportunities largely unexplored.
AI.method: LENS analyzes stakeholder interview transcripts using LLMs to both extract explicit requirements and infer latent (implicit) requirements by reasoning over stakeholder statements together with contextual information about organizational tools and infrastructure. Requirements are represented as user stories and linked to transcript excerpts for traceability.
AI.result: LENS achieved an average F1-score of 84.4% for extracting explicit requirements. On average, 75% of the latent requirements identified by LENS were perceived as providing useful automation or time-saving potential by domain experts.
AI.conclusion: LENS effectively extracts explicit requirements and uncovers latent requirements from stakeholder interviews, with domain experts validating the practical utility of the inferred needs, suggesting promise for AI-augmented requirements elicitation.

title: Federated Survival Analysis in Healthcare: A Multi-Model Evaluation on Cross-Institutional Heterogeneous Breast Cancer Data
summary: Survival analysis is central to clinical decision-making, yet reliable time-to-event models require large, diverse cohorts that are rarely available at a single institution, while privacy regulations restrict the centralization of patient data. Federated learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative by training shared models without exchanging raw data, but its effectiveness for survival modeling under realistic, heterogeneous conditions remains insufficiently understood. This paper presents a systematic, multi-model evaluation of federated survival analysis on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort with naturally heterogeneous distributed clients. Three representative survival models, the Cox Proportional Hazards model, DeepSurv, and Random Survival Forest (RSF), are compared across centralized, local, and federated training, and three federated optimization strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, and FedAdam) are assessed for the gradient-based models. Results show that FL consistently outperforms local training and approaches, and occasionally exceeds, centralized performance, while RSF offers the best overall balance of discrimination, calibration, and robustness across heterogeneous clients. We further find that performance depends on the diversity of client distributions, and that FedAvg and FedProx are stronger and more stable than FedAdam. Based on these findings, we derive practical, decision-oriented guidelines mapping data, privacy, interpretability, and resource constraints to recommended model and training-paradigm choices for federated survival modeling in healthcare.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates federated survival analysis on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort, comparing Cox, DeepSurv, and Random Survival Forest models under different federated optimization strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, FedAdam). Results show FL outperforms local training, RSF offers the best overall performance, and FedAvg/FedProx are more stable than FedAdam. Practical guidelines for model and training-paradigm selection are provided.
AI.motivation: Clinical survival analysis requires large, diverse cohorts that are often unavailable at single institutions, yet privacy regulations prevent centralizing patient data. Federated learning offers a privacy-preserving alternative, but its effectiveness for survival modeling under realistic heterogeneous conditions remains insufficiently understood.
AI.method: A systematic, multi-model evaluation of federated survival analysis on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort with naturally heterogeneous distributed clients. Three survival models (Cox Proportional Hazards, DeepSurv, Random Survival Forest) were compared across centralized, local, and federated training. Three federated optimization strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, FedAdam) were assessed for gradient-based models.
AI.result: FL consistently outperforms local training and approaches (sometimes exceeds) centralized performance. RSF offers the best overall balance of discrimination, calibration, and robustness. Performance depends on diversity of client distributions. FedAvg and FedProx are stronger and more stable than FedAdam.
AI.conclusion: The paper derives practical, decision-oriented guidelines mapping data, privacy, interpretability, and resource constraints to recommended model and training-paradigm choices for federated survival modeling in healthcare.

title: The Web4 Agent Economy: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of the Landscape, Challenges, and Opportunities
summary: The Internet is transitioning from Web3 toward Web4, where autonomous agents serve as independent economic actors. These agents can now hold crypto wallets, execute on-chain trades, and pay for external API calls. This transition calls for a new infrastructure stack capable of supporting key agent operations, including agent-to-tool interaction, agent-to-agent payments, and verifiable agent identity, represented by emerging protocols such as the Model Context Protocol, x402, and EIP-8004. Despite growing industrial interest in these protocols, the real-world Web4 agent ecosystem remains largely underexplored. To bridge this gap, we conduct the first large-scale empirical study of the Web4 ecosystem. Specifically, our study targets three interconnected questions: how Web4 agents are deployed and used in practice; what engineering challenges developers face when building Web4 agents; how current project communities respond to these challenges. To answer these questions, we analyze 99,448 multi-chain identity registrations, 317,596,323 transaction logs, the source code of 341 MCP projects, and 349 filtered GitHub issues. Our findings reveal that autonomous agents have established a highly active machine-to-machine payment economy, processing millions of daily transactions. However, this growth is built on immature infrastructure, including identity/authorization practice, cross-environment operation, and payment interoperability. Our follow-up analysis shows that community responses are visible but unevenly distributed across repositories, and payment interoperability remains the most persistent unresolved bottleneck. Overall, this study reveals a critical gap between the rapid growth of the Web4 agent economy and its fragile underlying infrastructure, highlighting future directions for building a more secure Web4 agent ecosystem.

AI.tldr: First large-scale empirical study of the Web4 agent ecosystem, analyzing 99K+ identity registrations, 317M+ transaction logs, 341 MCP projects, and 349 GitHub issues. Reveals a highly active machine-to-machine payment economy processing millions of daily transactions, but built on immature infrastructure with critical gaps in identity/authorization, cross-environment operation, and payment interoperability.
AI.motivation: The Web4 ecosystem (autonomous agents as independent economic actors with wallets, on-chain trading, and API payments) is rapidly emerging with new protocols (MCP, x402, EIP-8004), yet remains largely underexplored empirically. This study bridges that gap.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical analysis of 99,448 multi-chain identity registrations, 317,596,323 transaction logs, source code from 341 MCP projects, and 349 filtered GitHub issues across three research questions: deployment/usage patterns, engineering challenges, and community responses.
AI.result: Autonomous agents have established a highly active machine-to-machine payment economy (millions of daily transactions), but growth is built on immature infrastructure—problematic identity/authorization practices, cross-environment operation issues, and payment interoperability challenges. Community responses are visible but unevenly distributed, with payment interoperability remaining the most persistent bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: There is a critical gap between the rapid growth of the Web4 agent economy and its fragile infrastructure, highlighting the need for more secure infrastructure to support sustainable ecosystem development.

title: MGI: Member vs Generated Inference
summary: As generative models increasingly produce samples that are indistinguishable from human-created content, it becomes difficult to determine whether a given data point was part of a model's natural training set or was generated by the model itself, especially when models memorize and reproduce training data. We formalize this challenge as Member vs Generated Inference (MGI): given a sample and a target generative model, infer whether the sample is a true training member or a generated output of that model. Focusing on image generation, we show that existing membership inference methods systematically misclassify generated samples as training members, while attribution-based methods often misclassify true members as generated. This failure arises because both approaches rely on likelihood-related signals that are similarly elevated for training examples and for the model's own outputs. To address MGI, we propose Data Circuit Breaker (DCB), a three-stage method that combines complementary signals from a generative model's autoencoder and latent generator to distinguish training members from generated samples. Across multiple generative models, including image autoregressive and diffusion models, DCB consistently addresses the shortcomings of membership inference and attribution methods, remains effective even when models reproduce near-duplicates of training samples, and generalizes to challenging model derivative settings in which new models are trained on generated data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Member vs Generated Inference (MGI), showing that existing methods fail to distinguish training members from generated samples due to reliance on likelihood signals. The proposed Data Circuit Breaker (DCB) method leverages complementary signals from autoencoder and latent generator to effectively address this challenge across various generative models and derivative settings.
AI.motivation: As generative models produce outputs indistinguishable from human-created content, it becomes difficult to determine whether a sample was a training member or generated by the model itself, especially when models memorize and reproduce training data. Existing membership inference and attribution methods systematically fail at this task.
AI.method: Data Circuit Breaker (DCB), a three-stage method that combines complementary signals from a generative model's autoencoder and latent generator to distinguish training members from generated samples.
AI.result: DCB consistently addresses the shortcomings of membership inference and attribution methods across multiple generative models (image autoregressive and diffusion models), remains effective even when models reproduce near-duplicates of training samples, and generalizes to model derivative settings where new models are trained on generated data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DCB method effectively solves the Member vs Generated Inference (MGI) challenge by leveraging complementary signals from the autoencoder and latent generator, outperforming existing approaches across diverse generative models and challenging settings.

title: The Dependency Black Hole
comment: Accepted at the 23rd International Conference on Software Architecture, ICSA 26
summary: Microservice architectures promise independent evolution through loose coupling, yet large systems often exhibit strong dependency concentration around a small set of services. In an exploratory industrial case study of a product composed of 267 microservices, we triangulated multiple dependency signals -- compile-time, run-time, and task dependencies -- and iteratively validated our interpretations with practitioners. We observed a recurring macro-structure in the dependency network that resembles a black hole: a dense core of dependency magnets, a transitional region of services increasingly entangled with the core, and an outer region of lightly connected services. Based on these observations, we propose the dependency black hole theory, mapping the network to the black hole anatomy of a singularity, an event horizon, and an accretion disk, and formulating three hypotheses about how dependency concentration emerges and evolves at scale. The theory provides an explanatory lens for reasoning about dependency growth, identifying services at risk of becoming dependency magnets, and motivating governance interventions. We outline practical implications and directions for longitudinal and multi-case validation.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a "dependency black hole" pattern in large microservice systems, where dependency concentration forms a dense core (magnet services), a transitional region, and a periphery, analogous to a black hole's singularity, event horizon, and accretion disk.
AI.motivation: To understand how dependency concentration emerges and evolves in large microservice architectures, despite the promise of loose coupling, as observed in an industrial case study of 267 microservices.
AI.method: Exploratory industrial case study using triangulation of compile-time, run-time, and task dependency signals, with iterative validation by practitioners.
AI.result: Discovered a recurring macro-structure in the dependency network resembling a black hole: a dense core of dependency magnets, a transitional region, and an outer region; formulated the dependency black hole theory mapping to black hole anatomy (singularity, event horizon, accretion disk).
AI.conclusion: The theory explains dependency growth, helps identify services at risk of becoming dependency magnets, and motivates governance interventions; future work includes longitudinal and multi-case validation.

title: GRACE: Gated Refinement for Accurate Causal Edge Discovery in High-Dimensional Time Series
summary: From climate teleconnections to gene regulation, modern time-series datasets encompass tens or hundreds of interacting variables, making causal discovery increasingly challenging. Constraint-based methods offer statistical rigor but their nonlinear CI tests are infeasible at scale, while score-based alternatives avoid CI testing but require arbitrary thresholds to binarize continuous edge scores. We propose GRACE ($\textbf{G}$ated $\textbf{R}$efinement for $\textbf{A}$ccurate $\textbf{C}$ausal $\textbf{E}$dge discovery), which refines constraint-based discovery using Hard Concrete gates with $L_0$ regularization: each candidate edge has an independent gate whose values concentrate near 0 or 1, yielding a clean bimodal separation that makes the binary decision robust, unlike the narrow, overlapping score distributions produced by $L_1$ and attention-based methods. A fast linear CI skeleton provides high-recall candidates; a single gated model then prunes false positives by learning which edges genuinely improve prediction, with automatic regularization adapted to problem dimensions and skeleton density. Systematic experiments on synthetic benchmarks, spanning diverse graph topologies (scale-free, Erdős-R'enyi, small-world) and dimensionalities up to $d=100$, show that GRACE substantially improves F1 over its base CI method while maintaining high precision, and outperforms attention-based and score-based alternatives. GRACE matches or exceeds expensive nonlinear CI tests at a fraction of the cost ($75\times$ faster). On a real-world river flow dataset, where rainfall confounders, variable propagation lags, and distributional shifts violate standard assumptions, a temporal bootstrap variant of GRACE recovers 9 of 11 causal edges along the Elbe River with only 1 false positive ($F_1 = 0.86$, AUROC${} = 0.99$), reducing the skeleton's 106 false positives by 99%.

AI.tldr: GRACE refines constraint-based causal discovery using Hard Concrete gates with L0 regularization to binarize edge decisions cleanly, combining a fast linear CI skeleton with a gated pruning model. It achieves 75× speedup over nonlinear CI tests with comparable or better F1, and on a real river flow dataset recovers 9/11 causal edges with 99% false positive reduction.
AI.motivation: Constraint-based causal discovery methods with nonlinear CI tests are computationally infeasible at scale for high-dimensional time-series data, while score-based alternatives require arbitrary thresholds for binarizing continuous edge scores due to overlapping distributions from L1 and attention-based methods.
AI.method: GRACE uses Hard Concrete gates with L0 regularization on candidate edges, producing a bimodal distribution (concentrated near 0 or 1) for robust binary edge decisions. It first builds a high-recall candidate skeleton via fast linear CI tests, then trains a single gated model to prune false positives by assessing which edges genuinely improve prediction, with automatic regularization adapted to problem dimensions and skeleton density.
AI.result: On synthetic benchmarks (scale-free, Erdős-Rényi, small-world topologies, d≤100), GRACE substantially improves F1 over its base CI method with high precision, outperforms attention-based and score-based alternatives, and matches/exceeds expensive nonlinear CI tests at 75× speedup. On the Elbe River dataset, it recovers 9/11 causal edges (F1=0.86, AUROC=0.99), reducing 106 false positives by 99%.
AI.conclusion: GRACE provides scalable, accurate causal discovery for high-dimensional time-series by combining the statistical rigor of constraint-based methods with robust, automatic edge binarization via gated refinement, achieving state-of-the-art performance at dramatically reduced computational cost.

title: Helpful or Harmful? Evaluating LLM-Assisted Vulnerability Patching via a Human Study
summary: Software vulnerability remediation is a cognitively demanding task that requires specialized security expertise often lacking in general developers. In the meantime, Large Language Models (LLMs) assisted tools show potential in vulnerability detection, location, and repair tasks. [Hypothesis:] While LLM-assistance is hypothesized to accelerate patching, it also risks introducing hallucinations or insecure code, leading to a higher likelihood of generating superficial repairs that bypass the standard functionality checks but fail the security validation. [Objective:] We aim to present an empirical experiment, unveiling the capability of LLM-assisted vulnerability patching compared to manual debugging on human participants in real-world scenarios. [Method:] We plan to conduct a controlled experiment using a Balanced Crossover design. For that, we have developed a WebApp for code execution and integrated hidden Ghost Tests to verify patch integrity beyond visible functional requirements. The experiment involves training and evaluation scenarios. The remediation speed, remediation efficacy for both standard functionality tests and security tests, and participant perception will be evaluated. [Pilot Study:] A pilot experiment with a small sample of participants has been conducted, providing insights for the following study.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a controlled experiment (Balanced Crossover design) with hidden Ghost Tests to compare LLM-assisted vs. manual vulnerability patching, measuring remediation speed, efficacy (functionality & security), and participant perception. A pilot study has been completed.
AI.motivation: Software vulnerability remediation is cognitively demanding and requires specialized security expertise that general developers often lack. LLM-assisted tools show potential but risk introducing hallucinations or insecure code that bypass functionality checks while failing security validation.
AI.method: A controlled experiment using a Balanced Crossover design, with a WebApp for code execution and integrated hidden Ghost Tests to verify patch integrity beyond visible functional requirements. The experiment includes training and evaluation scenarios.
AI.result: A pilot experiment with a small sample of participants has been conducted, providing preliminary insights for the following larger study.
AI.conclusion: The study will evaluate remediation speed, remediation efficacy (for both standard functionality tests and security tests), and participant perception to compare LLM-assisted vulnerability patching against manual debugging.

title: ARIA: Adaptive Region-Based Importance Allocation for Conditional Diffusion Distillation
summary: Distilling conditional diffusion models aims to transfer the behavior of a large teacher to a smaller student while preserving alignment across conditioning inputs. Unlike recognition tasks, knowledge distillation in conditional diffusion often struggles to transfer knowledge beyond the training distribution, since the predicted noise strongly depends on the conditioning signal. As a result, effective distillation requires exploring a large conditioning space. In practical settings, this creates a major bottleneck. Paired image-condition data may be limited, and generating synthetic images for every available condition is often computationally infeasible, while the pool of conditions, such as text prompts, can be extremely large. Recent work addresses this issue by switching conditions during training, exposing the student to a broader conditioning space without changing the distillation objective. Yet this raises a complementary question: once a large conditioning corpus is available, how should the training effort be allocated? In this work, we introduce ARIA, a framework that adaptively allocates training effort across coarse regions of the conditioning space. By maintaining online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy at the region level, ARIA focuses updates where misalignment persists while preserving the original distillation objective. Empirically, ARIA improves over RC across most architectures and settings, with the clearest gains observed in unseen and underrepresented regimes. We also provide a theoretical analysis showing that the proposed tracking mechanism follows the evolving discrepancy during training under bounded variance and drift assumptions.

AI.tldr: ARIA adaptively allocates training effort across regions of the conditioning space in conditional diffusion distillation, using online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy to focus updates where misalignment persists, improving performance particularly on unseen and underrepresented conditions.
AI.motivation: Conditional diffusion distillation struggles to transfer knowledge beyond the training distribution because predicted noise strongly depends on conditioning signals. Existing methods (like switching conditions) expose students to broader conditioning spaces but do not address how to optimally allocate training effort across a large conditioning corpus.
AI.method: ARIA (Adaptive Region-level Iterative Allocation) maintains online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy at the coarse region level of the conditioning space, then adaptively allocates training effort to regions where misalignment persists, while preserving the original distillation objective.
AI.result: ARIA improves over RC (Random Conditioning) across most architectures and settings, with the clearest gains in unseen and underrepresented conditioning regimes. Theoretical analysis shows the tracking mechanism follows evolving discrepancy under bounded variance and drift assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive allocation of training effort based on teacher-student discrepancy is an effective strategy for improving conditional diffusion distillation, particularly for handling large conditioning spaces where data is limited or conditions are diverse.

title: Closing the Loop: Formally Verified Law as a Reward Signal for Self-Improving Legal AI
summary: This article develops an architecture that creates a formally verifiable reward signal to train legal AI, adapting the LLM proposes, verifier disposes paradigm from mathematical AI to the distinctive demands of law. We present an architecture comprising LLM-driven autoformalization into a formal legal calculus extending Catala, a verification kernel, and explanation generation grounded in formal proof traces. For the computational components of law, the architecture provides provable correctness. For open-textured legal analysis, it provides structural guarantees: every required stage of the legal argument is addressed, argumentation is exercised at the correct stages and not omitted, and the deductive links between steps are valid. We demonstrate the architecture on procedural deadline calculations in German law, Commerce Clause analysis in U.S. constitutional law, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality. We further show that the same architecture has a structural advantage for legal AI training: a deterministic external verifier supplies verifiable outcomes for legal problems and thereby closes the traditional reinforcement-learning loop gap in law.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a verifiable legal AI architecture based on the "LLM proposes, verifier disposes" paradigm, combining LLM-driven autoformalization into Catala-based formal legal calculus, a verification kernel, and explanation generation from proof traces. It provides provable correctness for computational legal components and structural guarantees for open-textured analysis, demonstrated on German procedural deadlines, U.S. Commerce Clause, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality, while also showing the architecture's advantage for legal AI training via deterministic external verifiers that close the reinforcement learning loop gap.
AI.motivation: To create a formally verifiable reward signal for training legal AI by adapting mathematical AI verification paradigms to the distinctive demands of law, addressing the lack of verifiable outcomes in legal AI training (the reinforcement learning loop gap).
AI.method: An architecture with three components: (1) LLM-driven autoformalization of legal text into a formal legal calculus extending Catala, (2) a verification kernel that checks correctness, and (3) explanation generation grounded in formal proof traces. This follows the "LLM proposes, verifier disposes" paradigm adapted from mathematical AI.
AI.result: Demonstrated on three legal domains: procedural deadline calculations in German law, Commerce Clause analysis in U.S. constitutional law, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality. The architecture provides provable correctness for computational law components and structural guarantees (complete argument stages, correct-stage reasoning, valid deductive links) for open-textured analysis.
AI.conclusion: The same architecture offers a structural advantage for legal AI training by providing a deterministic external verifier that supplies verifiable outcomes for legal problems, thereby closing the traditional reinforcement-learning loop gap in law.

title: Catastrophic Compositional Generation: Why Vanilla Diffusion Models Fail to Extrapolate
summary: The task of compositional generation involves using a conditional generative model, trained only on a subset of the possible conditions, to produce samples from compositionally-defined target distributions such as a geometric combination of the source distributions. In this work, we argue that this task is often infeasible for vanilla conditional diffusion models: we conjecture that no inference-time technique can efficiently produce samples from the target distribution in certain well-motivated settings. This idea is supported by theory-guided generalization arguments and carefully-designed experiments on both synthetic and realistic data. In particular, while recent methods such as Feynman-Kac correction reduce inference-time approximation error, our results show that score estimation error has a more catastrophic effect on performance when the target distribution is out-of-distribution with respect to the sources, highlighting the need for a different approach to this task.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that compositional generation with vanilla conditional diffusion models is often infeasible due to score estimation errors, especially when the target distribution is out-of-distribution relative to the training sources, and that no inference-time technique can fully overcome this limitation.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether vanilla conditional diffusion models can effectively perform compositional generation — producing samples from geometrically combined target distributions using only a subset of conditions during training.
AI.method: Theoretical generalization arguments combined with carefully designed experiments on synthetic and realistic data, comparing inference-time techniques like Feynman-Kac correction against score estimation error effects.
AI.result: Score estimation error has a catastrophic effect on out-of-distribution target distributions, while inference-time methods like Feynman-Kac correction only reduce approximation error but cannot fix fundamental feasibility issues.
AI.conclusion: Compositional generation is often infeasible for vanilla conditional diffusion models, highlighting the need for alternative approaches beyond inference-time corrections.

title: KLip-PPO: A per-sample KL perspective on PPO-Clip
summary: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is the standard policy-gradient algorithm for on-policy reinforcement learning. The literature presents it in two forms, a clipped surrogate that bounds the importance ratio between successive policies and a Kullback-Leibler penalty between them. These forms are treated as separate algorithms with their own gradients, their own hyperparameters, and their own reference implementations, and a sizeable body of empirical work compares them. We show that the gradient of the clipped surrogate is reproduced exactly by a Kullback-Leibler surrogate whose coefficient varies per sample, with closed-form dependence on the importance ratio and the advantage. The identity holds at every minibatch step and across the entire inner loop, and on five MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks the two losses produce indistinguishable training curves. The reformulation exposes a structural feature of the clipped surrogate that the min notation hides. PPO-Clip's implicit per-sample penalty is a step function at the boundary of the trust region, and the shape of this coefficient is the natural design axis for generalising the algorithm. We sketch the resulting follow-up directions in the discussion.

AI.tldr: PPO's clipped surrogate and Kullback-Leibler penalty forms are not separate algorithms; the clipped surrogate's gradient is exactly reproduced by a per-sample adaptive KL penalty with closed-form dependence on importance ratio and advantage, producing indistinguishable training curves on MuJoCo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The literature treats PPO's clipped surrogate and KL penalty as distinct algorithms with separate gradients, hyperparameters, and implementations, leading to extensive but potentially misguided empirical comparisons. The authors aim to reveal their fundamental equivalence.
AI.method: The authors derive analytically that PPO-Clip's gradient matches a per-sample adaptive KL penalty surrogate with closed-form coefficient depending on importance ratio and advantage. They validate empirically on five MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks by comparing training curves of the two losses.
AI.result: The gradient identity holds at every minibatch step across the entire inner loop. On five MuJoCo benchmarks, clipped and KL surrogates produce indistinguishable training curves, confirming theoretical equivalence.
AI.conclusion: PPO-Clip implicitly enforces a per-sample penalty that is a step function at the trust-region boundary. The coefficient's shape, hidden by the min notation, is the natural design axis for generalizing PPO, opening follow-up directions.

title: DREG: A Layer-Wise Jacobian Regularization as a General-Purpose Penalty
summary: We present a large-scale empirical study isolating the contributions of the Derivative Regularization penalty (DREG). Across a fully-crossed factorial sweep of 960 experiments spanning 4 activations, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, and 5 random seeds, we ask: when, where, and why does DREG work? Our results establish three principal findings. First, DREG achieves the highest overall and clean-regime accuracy among all regularizers evaluated (significantly so against the unregularized baseline, Weight Decay, and IGPen; Wilcoxon $p \leq 0.031$). It ranks second in noise robustness behind Spectral Normalization (SN) - the only two layer-wise regularizers in the study. Second, DREG is globally the best-performing regularizer under GELU, the default activation in modern transformer architectures, particularly on both messy vision and messy NLP benchmarks, suggesting direct applicability to frontier deep learning settings. Third, DREG's advantage over competing regularizers is most pronounced under data scarcity, consistent with its role as a geometric inductive bias that substitutes for the regularizing effect of data volume. Throughout, DREG is applied with a single fixed hyperparameter $λ= 10^{-2.5}$ and no per-dataset tuning, supporting its characterization as a plug-and-play regularizer for neural networks with nontrivial Jacobian structure. These findings are consistent with DREG's design: concentrating regularization pressure on layers where the activation derivative is largest, rather than constraining the network uniformly.

AI.tldr: DREG (Derivative Regularization) is a plug-and-play regularizer that achieves top accuracy, ranks second in noise robustness, excels under GELU activations and data scarcity, and works with a fixed hyperparameter across 960 experiments.
AI.motivation: To isolate and characterize the contributions of Derivative Regularization (DREG) by systematically answering when, where, and why it works across diverse experimental conditions.
AI.method: A fully-crossed factorial sweep of 960 experiments spanning 4 activations, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, and 5 random seeds, comparing DREG against baselines including unregularized, Weight Decay, IGPen, and Spectral Normalization.
AI.result: (1) DREG achieves highest overall and clean-regime accuracy (significant vs. baselines; Wilcoxon p≤0.031), and ranks second in noise robustness behind SN; (2) DREG is globally best under GELU, especially on messy vision and NLP benchmarks; (3) DREG's advantage is most pronounced under data scarcity.
AI.conclusion: DREG serves as an effective plug-and-play regularizer for neural networks with nontrivial Jacobian structure, concentrating regularization pressure on layers with largest activation derivatives rather than constraining uniformly.

title: Learning the Koopman Operator using Attention Free Transformers
summary: Learning Koopman operators with autoencoders enables linear prediction in a latent space, but long-horizon rollouts often drift off the learned manifold, leading to phase and amplitude errors on systems with switching, continuous spectra, or strong transients. We introduce two complementary components that make Koopman predictors more robust. First, we add an attention-free latent memory (AFT) block that aggregates a short window of past latents to produce a corrected latent before each Koopman update. Unlike multi-head attention, AFT operates in linear time and adds only $\approx$30k parameters ($3d^2 + T^2$, fewer than matched multi-head attention), yet captures the local temporal context needed to suppress error divergence. Second, we propose dynamic re-encoding: lightweight, online change-point triggers (EWMA, CUSUM, and sequential two-sample tests) that detect latent drift and project predictions back onto the autoencoder manifold. Across three benchmark systems -- Duffing oscillator, Repressilator, IRMA -- our model consistently reduces error accumulation compared to a Koopman autoencoder and matched-capacity multi-head attention. We also compare against GRU and Transformer autoencoders, evaluated both from initial conditions and with a 50-step context, and find that Koopman+AFT (with optional re-encoding) attains markedly lower long-horizon error while maintaining lower inference latency. We report improvements over horizons up to 1000 steps, together with ablations over trigger policies. The result is a fast, compact predictor that stays on the learned manifold over long horizons.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces two components—attention-free latent memory (AFT) blocks and dynamic re-encoding with change-point detection—to stabilize Koopman autoencoder predictions over long horizons, demonstrating reduced error accumulation on benchmark dynamical systems.
AI.motivation: Koopman autoencoders enable linear latent-space prediction but suffer from drift off the learned manifold over long horizons, causing phase/amplitude errors especially on systems with switching, continuous spectra, or strong transients.
AI.method: (1) AFT block: attention-free, linear-time module that aggregates past latents to produce a corrected latent before each Koopman update (~30k params, 3d²+T²). (2) Dynamic re-encoding: lightweight online change-point triggers (EWMA, CUSUM, sequential two-sample tests) that detect latent drift and project predictions back onto the autoencoder manifold.
AI.result: On Duffing oscillator, Repressilator, and IRMA benchmarks, Koopman+AFT with optional re-encoding achieves markedly lower long-horizon error than Koopman autoencoder, matched multi-head attention, GRU, and Transformer baselines, with lower inference latency; improvements hold over 1000-step horizons.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach yields a fast, compact predictor that stays on the learned manifold over long horizons, with ablations validating the trigger policy design.

title: Forget Without Compromise: Nexus Sampling for Streaming KV-Cache Eviction Under Fixed Budgets
summary: Long-context and agentic LLM workloads push the KV cache past any fixed memory budget, forcing the inference stack to permanently evict tokens at every step of a continuous-inference stream. Existing methods all share the same template, a per-step direct-attention score followed by deterministic top-$K$ selection, which converts a single below-cutoff step into an irreversible verdict and permanently erases any subtly important token that direct attention cannot single out from noise. To address this challenge, we propose Nexus Sampling, a training-free eviction method that pairs Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention that surfaces bridge tokens, with weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens with inclusion probability in place of deterministic top-$K$. Theoretically, we show that Nexus Sampling dominates deterministic top-$K$ in long-run survival of subtly important tokens. Empirically, at 80% KV cache eviction, Nexus Sampling matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench while outperforming top-$K$ baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, with up to 10x smaller per-sequence cache memory.

AI.tldr: Nexus Sampling is a training-free KV cache eviction method that uses iterative Nexus scoring and weighted reservoir sampling to retain subtly important tokens, outperforming deterministic top-K baselines on long-context and retrieval-heavy tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing deterministic top-K eviction methods make irreversible eviction decisions based on per-step direct-attention scores, which can permanently discard subtly important tokens that are indistinguishable from noise at a single step. This is especially problematic for long-context and agentic LLM workloads where the KV cache exceeds fixed memory budgets.
AI.method: Nexus Sampling combines two components: (1) Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention scores to surface "bridge tokens" (tokens that are indirectly important), and (2) weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens probabilistically based on inclusion probability rather than deterministic top-K selection.
AI.result: At 80% KV cache eviction, Nexus Sampling matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench, outperforms top-K baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, and achieves up to 10x smaller per-sequence cache memory.
AI.conclusion: Nexus Sampling theoretically dominates deterministic top-K in long-run survival of subtly important tokens, empirically achieving strong performance across both long-context understanding and retrieval-intensive tasks with significant memory savings.

title: 3D Masked Autoencoders are Robust Learners of Volumetric and Multimodal Cellular Representations for Microscopy
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026. Code available at: https://github.com/marrlab/mae3d-opencell
summary: Self-supervised learning in fluorescence microscopy often relies on 2D projections, despite the inherently three-dimensional nature of cells. We present a systematic comparison of 2D and 3D masked autoencoders (MAE-2D vs. MAE-3D) on volumetric microscopy data. Under matched architectures and training protocols, MAE-3D consistently outperforms 2D max-projection and slice-based variants on downstream single-cell tasks. We further align visual representations with a pretrained protein language model (ESM2) and show that cross-modal supervision yields larger gains for volumetric models. Channel cross-attention and frequency-domain regularization are critical for leveraging 3D spatial context. On a protein--protein interaction task, MAE-3D achieves a ROC--AUC of 0.865, outperforming prior methods by up to +0.025. For protein localization, our best 3D model attains state-of-the-art AUC$_{\text{micro}}$ (0.952) and F1$_{\text{micro}}$ (0.742), improving over previous approaches by +0.003 and +0.010 absolute, respectively. Overall, these results demonstrate the advantages of native 3D modeling and multimodal alignment for representation learning in single-cell microscopy.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares 2D and 3D masked autoencoders (MAE) on volumetric fluorescence microscopy data, showing that 3D MAE consistently outperforms 2D variants on downstream tasks. Cross-modal alignment with a protein language model (ESM2) further improves volumetric models, achieving state-of-the-art results on protein-protein interaction (ROC-AUC 0.865) and protein localization (AUC_micro 0.952, F1_micro 0.742) tasks.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised learning in fluorescence microscopy predominantly uses 2D projections despite cells being inherently three-dimensional, creating a gap between representation learning approaches and the actual volumetric nature of biological data.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of MAE-2D vs. MAE-3D under matched architectures and training protocols on volumetric microscopy data. Cross-modal alignment with pretrained ESM2 protein language model. Key architectural components include channel cross-attention and frequency-domain regularization.
AI.result: MAE-3D outperforms 2D max-projection and slice-based variants. Cross-modal supervision yields larger gains for volumetric models. On protein-protein interaction: ROC-AUC 0.865 (+0.025 over prior). On protein localization: AUC_micro 0.952 (+0.003), F1_micro 0.742 (+0.010), both state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: Native 3D modeling and multimodal alignment are advantageous for representation learning in single-cell microscopy, demonstrating that volumetric architectures better capture spatial context inherent to cellular biology.

title: A Comparative Study of Bayesian Contextual Bandits for Real-Time Warehouse Sorter Optimization
comment: Accepted at 2026 IEEE International Conference on Mechatronics and Automation (IEEE ICMA 2026)
summary: Efficient sorter diversion control of automated material handling systems (MHS) is critical for optimizing operational efficiency in large-scale warehouse environments. In this study, we use an inbound receiving sorter at a high-volume e-commerce warehouse as our primary use case, where the sorter diversion system relies on cost functions with static weight configurations that fail to adapt to highly dynamic system contexts, such as volume mode, congestion level, equipment physical status, and upstream/downstream dependencies. To address this real-time sorter diversion optimization challenge, we conducted a comparative study of three candidate hybrid machine learning frameworks: Linear Regression with Gradient Descent Optimization (LR+GDO), XGBoost with Bayesian Optimization (XGB+BO), and Bayesian Contextual Bandits (BCB). Model training and evaluation were enabled by leveraging a high-fidelity physics-aware emulator to overcome the cold-start problem and allow a safe transition from offline to online learning. We performed comprehensive evaluations including reward model predictive accuracy, contextual sensitivity, action distribution, and projected reward uplift.
