Our results demonstrate that while tree-based reward models offer slightly better predictive power, the BCB framework achieved overall higher performance with 2.03% reward uplift over the heuristic baseline. Furthermore, BCB exhibits several superior characteristics, such as its decisive time-optimal policy backed by Bang-Bang control theory, continuous online learning capability, strategic balance between exploration and exploitation, and significantly shorter inference latency. These results demonstrate the potential of the BCB framework for real-time control optimization in large-scale warehouse environments, motivating further investigation toward operational deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper compares three hybrid ML frameworks (LR+GDO, XGB+BO, BCB) for real-time sorter diversion control in automated MHS at e-commerce warehouses, finding Bayesian Contextual Bandits (BCB) achieves 2.03% reward uplift over baseline with superior inference speed and online learning capability.
AI.motivation: Static weight configurations in sorter diversion cost functions fail to adapt to dynamic system contexts (e.g., volume mode, congestion, equipment status, upstream/downstream dependencies), necessitating a real-time optimization solution for inbound receiving sorters in high-volume e-commerce warehouses.
AI.method: A comparative study of three hybrid ML frameworks—Linear Regression with Gradient Descent Optimization (LR+GDO), XGBoost with Bayesian Optimization (XGB+BO), and Bayesian Contextual Bandits (BCB)—trained using a high-fidelity physics-aware emulator to enable safe offline-to-online transition and address the cold-start problem.
AI.result: BCB achieved 2.03% reward uplift over the heuristic baseline. While tree-based reward models (XGB+BO) offered slightly better predictive accuracy, BCB demonstrated superior overall performance with decisive time-optimal policy (Bang-Bang control), continuous online learning, strategic exploration-exploitation balance, and significantly lower inference latency.
AI.conclusion: The BCB framework shows strong potential for real-time control optimization in large-scale warehouse environments, motivating further investigation toward operational deployment.

title: Offline Reinforcement Learning for Warehouse SLAM Throughput Control
comment: Accepted at 2026 14th International Conference on Control, Mechatronics and Automation (ICCMA 2026)
summary: We present an offline reinforcement learning (RL) framework for optimizing SLAM throughput control in a warehouse fulfillment environment. SLAM (Scan/Label/Apply/Manifest) throughput directly influences system congestion and operational efficiency. Our RL-based control approach dynamically recommends SLAM throughput settings that adaptively balance throughput maximization with downstream stability through intelligent adjustment of throttling behavior. We include a history-informed state representation, action space abstraction for delayed-impact control, and a reward function that captures both upstream and downstream operational metrics. Our approach is algorithm-agnostic, enabling integration of multiple offline RL methods under a unified architecture. We instantiate our framework with three state-of-the-art offline RL algorithms, and trained the models offline using de-identified historical operational logs from a large-scale warehouse. Policy performance is evaluated using a comprehensive multi-method strategy. These include model-free approaches including immediate reward estimation via regression models and long-horizon Fitted Q Evaluation (FQE), as well as model-based Deep Koopman dynamics evaluation. Empirical results reveal that the CQL policy consistently outperforms alternatives, improving system health by 22.97% and reducing average throttling duration by 3.18%. These findings demonstrate the potential of offline RL for safe and scalable warehouse throughput control optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an offline RL framework for optimizing SLAM throughput control in warehouse fulfillment, using history-informed state, delayed-impact action abstraction, and multi-metric rewards. Three offline RL algorithms are evaluated via model-free and model-based methods; CQL achieves 22.97% system health improvement and 3.18% throttling duration reduction.
AI.motivation: SLAM throughput directly influences system congestion and operational efficiency in warehouse fulfillment environments, motivating the need for an adaptive control approach that balances throughput maximization with downstream stability.
AI.method: The framework includes a history-informed state representation, action space abstraction for delayed-impact control, and a reward function capturing both upstream and downstream operational metrics. It is algorithm-agnostic, enabling integration of multiple offline RL methods (e.g., CQL, other SOTA algorithms). Training uses de-identified historical operational logs from a large-scale warehouse. Evaluation employs model-free approaches (immediate reward estimation via regression, long-horizon Fitted Q Evaluation) and model-based Deep Koopman dynamics evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical results show the CQL policy consistently outperforms alternatives, improving system health by 22.97% and reducing average throttling duration by 3.18%.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate the potential of offline RL for safe and scalable warehouse throughput control optimization.

title: Learning to Trigger: Reinforcement Learning at the Large Hadron Collider
summary: High-throughput scientific facilities such as the Large Hadron Collider depend on real-time event filtering (\textit{triggering}) under tight constraints on bandwidth, latency, and storage. In practice, trigger menus are largely static and hand-tuned and can become suboptimal as detector conditions, pileup, and background composition drift over time. We cast online threshold tuning as a sequential decision-making problem: a reinforcement learning agent ingests streaming summaries of recent rates and signal-sensitive features and updates trigger thresholds to maximize signal efficiency while tracking a target background rate within a tolerance band. We adapt Group-Filtered Policy Optimization (GFPO) to streaming control and introduce two variants (GFPO-F, GFPO-FR) that enforce background rate feasibility during training. On a benchmark that emulates realistic collider operation, we study two representative triggers: a total transverse energy ($H_{T}$) trigger sensitive to pileup variation, and an anomaly-detection (AD) trigger based on reconstruction loss for rare or non-standard signatures. On Monte Carlo streams, our agent increases the fraction of in-tolerance time intervals by 48\% ($H_T$) and 28\% (AD), with a cumulative gain of up to 2\% in signal efficiency on those in-tolerance intervals. Transferring from simulation to \emph{real} collision data (CMS Run 283408), the same agent, without fine-tuning, achieves a 56\% ($H_T$) and 28\% (AD) in-tolerance improvement over baselines, with further signal-efficiency gain on both triggers. To our knowledge, this is the \emph{first} demonstration of RL-based trigger control on real Large Hadron Collider collision data. Code is available at https://github.com/Zixind/GFPO\_LHC.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first RL-based trigger threshold control system applied to real LHC collision data, using Group-Filtered Policy Optimization variants (GFPO-F, GFPO-FR) to dynamically adjust trigger thresholds. On CMS Run 283408 data, the agent improves in-tolerance time intervals by 56% (H_T) and 28% (AD) over baselines without fine-tuning, with up to 2% signal efficiency gain.
AI.motivation: Static hand-tuned trigger menus at high-throughput facilities like the LHC become suboptimal as detector conditions, pileup, and background composition drift over time, motivating the need for adaptive online trigger threshold tuning.
AI.method: The authors cast online threshold tuning as a sequential decision-making problem using reinforcement learning. They adapt Group-Filtered Policy Optimization (GFPO) to streaming control and introduce two variants (GFPO-F, GFPO-FR) that enforce background rate feasibility during training. The agent ingests streaming summaries of rates and signal-sensitive features to update trigger thresholds while maximizing signal efficiency and tracking a target background rate within a tolerance band.
AI.result: On Monte Carlo streams, the agent increases in-tolerance time intervals by 48% (H_T) and 28% (AD), with up to 2% cumulative signal efficiency gain. On real CMS Run 283408 data without fine-tuning, improvements are 56% (H_T) and 28% (AD) in-tolerance over baselines, with additional signal-efficiency gains on both triggers.
AI.conclusion: This is the first demonstration of RL-based trigger control on real LHC collision data, showing that adaptive threshold tuning via RL can significantly improve trigger performance under evolving conditions, transferring effectively from simulation to real data without fine-tuning.

title: EMAgnet: Parameter-Space EMA Regularization for Policy Gradient Self-Play in Large Games
comment: Accepted at NExT-Game 2026: New Frontiers in Game-Theoretic Learning (ICML 2026 Workshop). 13 pages, 2 figures,
summary: Recent work has established that regularized policy gradient methods such as PPO, when used in self-play, can match or exceed specialized game-theoretic algorithms for solving two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games. The uniform distribution has emerged as a strong policy regularization target for this purpose, but it regularizes equally toward all actions regardless of their viability. We introduce EMAgnet, which instead regularizes toward an exponential moving average (EMA) of the last-iterate policy's parameters, providing an adaptive regularization target that evolves with the agent's improving strategy. We evaluate EMAgnet on both standard two-player zero-sum benchmarks and modified benchmarks with exploration challenges and large numbers of strictly dominated strategies. Relative to PPO self-play with uniform-magnet regularization under both linear and power-law annealing schedules, EMAgnet achieves lower exploitability in the majority of tested environments, with consistent performance gains across games containing strictly dominated strategies.

AI.tldr: EMAgnet introduces an exponential moving average of the last-iterate policy's parameters as an adaptive regularization target for policy gradient self-play, achieving lower exploitability than uniform-magnet PPO in most two-player zero-sum games tested, especially those with dominated strategies.
AI.motivation: Uniform distribution as a regularization target in PPO self-play treats all actions equally regardless of viability, which is suboptimal. The authors aim to develop an adaptive regularization target that evolves with the agent's improving strategy.
AI.method: EMAgnet regularizes toward an exponential moving average (EMA) of the last-iterate policy's parameters instead of a fixed uniform distribution, providing an adaptive regularization target that evolves as the agent improves.
AI.result: EMAgnet achieves lower exploitability than PPO self-play with uniform-magnet regularization under both linear and power-law annealing schedules in the majority of tested environments, with consistent gains in games containing strictly dominated strategies.
AI.conclusion: Regularizing toward an EMA of the policy's own parameters is a more effective approach than uniform regularization for self-play policy gradient methods in two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games, particularly when exploration challenges or dominated strategies are present.

title: Cyclic Denoising Reveals Ultrastable Memories in Diffusion Models
comment: 22 pages, 7 main figures; supplementary material included. Supplementary movies available at the project webpage
summary: We introduce cyclic denoising -- repeated forward and reverse diffusion at controlled noise amplitudes -- as an extraction attack for image diffusion models. Inspired by random organization in disordered solids, cyclic denoising exposes regions of the learned distribution that are largely inaccessible to standard sampling. The dynamics drive samples toward attractors with a broad stability spectrum. The deepest attractors are ultrastable: they regenerate after near-total corruption and persist through thousands of noising-denoising cycles. Many of these attractors correspond to memorized training images, including stock photographs, brand watermarks, and web-crawl artifacts. The attack requires only sampler-level control, with no gradients, weight inspection, prompts, captions, or prior knowledge of the training data. Unlike generate-and-filter attacks, which rely on large-scale prompted generation and post-hoc similarity or membership-inference filtering, our main protocol is fully unconditioned. We demonstrate the phenomenon in Stable Diffusion v1.4 and in a pixel-space DDPM, showing consistent behavior across latent- and pixel-space diffusion models. Across noise amplitudes, we observe a yielding-like transition: low-amplitude cycling produces trivial absorbing fixed points or limit cycles, while larger amplitudes induce rearrangements, basin hopping, and long-lived trapping in structured memorized attractor basins. We also observe hierarchical partial absorption, prompt-stabilized basins, and cross-initial-condition universality of the recovered attractor set. Our results therefore show that cyclic denoising is both a physics-inspired probe of generative landscapes and a practical tool for memorization auditing, with implications for privacy, copyright compliance, and model fingerprinting.

AI.tldr: Cyclic denoising — repeated forward and reverse diffusion at controlled noise levels — is introduced as a gradient-free extraction attack on diffusion models. It reveals ultrastable attractors that correspond to memorized training images (e.g., stock photos, watermarks). The method works across latent and pixel-space models (Stable Diffusion v1.4, DDPM), shows a yielding-like transition across noise amplitudes, and serves as a probe of generative landscapes and a practical memorization auditing tool with privacy and copyright implications.
AI.motivation: To develop a physics-inspired, training-free method for exposing memorized training images in diffusion models, addressing privacy, copyright, and auditing concerns without requiring gradients, weight inspection, or prompts.
AI.method: Cyclic denoising: repeatedly applying forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) diffusion at controlled noise amplitudes to drive samples toward attractors. The dynamics are analyzed across amplitudes, revealing a yielding-like transition where larger amplitudes expose structured memorized attractor basins.
AI.result: Ultrastable attractors correspond to memorized training images (stock photographs, brand watermarks, web-crawl artifacts). The attack works unconditionally in Stable Diffusion v1.4 and pixel-space DDPM. Observations include hierarchical partial absorption, prompt-stabilized basins, and cross-initial-condition universality.
AI.conclusion: Cyclic denoising is both a physics-inspired probe of generative landscapes and a practical tool for memorization auditing, with implications for privacy, copyright compliance, and model fingerprinting.

title: Fast and Slow Variational Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning remains a major challenge for modern deep networks, partly because commonly used optimizers lack inherent mechanisms for continual adaptation. One such natural mechanism is fast and slow adaptation to balance stability and plasticity. This mechanism has deep roots in neuroscience and biology, but there is no consensus on how to best incorporate it in commonly used optimizers. Here, we show that this can be easily done via the VCL framework, where past posteriors are used as priors in the future. Our key idea is to incorporate slow adaptation via merging of past posteriors to slow down the drift in the knowledge as learning progresses. The merged posterior is then used as the prior in the VCL update to implement the fast-weight updates. These steps can be seamlessly implemented in the IVON optimizer, whose form and costs are nearly identical to that of Adam. We call this new optimizer the Continual IVON (CoVON) optimizer and show that it not only consistently improves over existing VCL optimizers, but also performs better than other weight-regularization strategies across domain-incremental learning, continual pre-training, and fine-tuning of large language models.

AI.tldr: CoVON is a new optimizer that integrates fast and slow adaptation into continual learning by merging past posteriors as priors within the VCL framework, implemented via the IVON optimizer, outperforming existing methods across domain-incremental learning, continual pre-training, and LLM fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Continual learning remains challenging for deep networks because standard optimizers lack inherent mechanisms for continual adaptation, particularly the balance between stability and plasticity that exists in biological learning systems.
AI.method: The authors incorporate slow adaptation by merging past posteriors (to slow knowledge drift) as priors in the VCL update (for fast-weight updates), implemented seamlessly in the IVON optimizer with similar form and cost to Adam.
AI.result: CoVON consistently improves over existing VCL optimizers and outperforms other weight-regularization strategies in domain-incremental learning, continual pre-training, and fine-tuning of large language models.
AI.conclusion: Fast and slow adaptation can be easily integrated into commonly used optimizers via the VCL framework with posterior merging, yielding a practical and effective continual learning optimizer (CoVON).

title: You Don't Need to Run Every Eval
summary: A modern model release reports scores on 40+ benchmarks and the same evaluations were run many more times before it: to track training progress, compare design choices, and select the checkpoint for the release. But do we need to run every eval? We compile a public score matrix of 84 frontier models on 133 benchmarks (2,604 cells, 23.3% filled) and find it is approximately rank-2: a model's scores across all 133 benchmarks are largely determined by just two numbers. We confirm this in two ways: scores hidden from the matrix are best recovered using two factors, and two factors already explain over 90% of the variation among models on the benchmarks they share. Building on this, we design BenchPress: a logit-space rank-2 matrix completion method that recovers held-out scores to within 4.6 points, and a confidence layer that says when each prediction can be trusted. Using BenchPress, we find a subset of five benchmarks {GPQA-D, HLE, Codeforces, MMLU-Pro, ARC-AGI-1} that can recover the rest of a model's public scorecard to within 3.93 points. For a tighter inference budget, a cheaper set {GPQA-D, MMLU-Pro, Aider Polyglot, MATH-500, AIME 2026} can predict a model's evals to within 4.55. We release the score matrix, the BenchPress code, and an interactive tool that predicts any model's score on any benchmark.

AI.tldr: A rank-2 structure in 84 frontier models' scores across 133 benchmarks allows predicting hidden scores with only two factors; BenchPress achieves recovery within ~4.6 points, and just five benchmarks suffice to reconstruct a model's full scorecard.
AI.motivation: The paper questions whether running all 40+ benchmarks for every model evaluation is necessary, given the redundancy and cost of extensive evaluation pipelines.
AI.method: The authors compile a sparse matrix (84 models × 133 benchmarks, 23.3% filled), demonstrate its approximately rank-2 structure, and design BenchPress — a logit-space rank-2 matrix completion method with a confidence layer for trustworthy predictions.
AI.result: BenchPress recovers held-out scores within 4.6 points; a subset of five benchmarks (GPQA-D, HLE, Codeforces, MMLU-Pro, ARC-AGI-1) reconstructs the full scorecard within 3.93 points; a cheaper set achieves within 4.55 points.
AI.conclusion: Model evaluation can be drastically compressed: only a handful of benchmarks are needed to infer a model's performance across a wide array of tests, reducing evaluation cost and effort.

title: Information-Theoretic Classifier-Free Guidance with Adaptive Schedule Optimization
summary: Diffusion models have achieved strong performance in image, text-to-image, and video generation, where conditional generation is often controlled by classifier-free guidance (CFG). CFG improves condition consistency by increasing a guidance weight, but stronger guidance typically reduces diversity and distributional coverage. It remains unclear how this consistency-coverage trade-off should be controlled across the reverse trajectory, since the distribution induced by CFG is not simply the fixed-time tilted distribution given by the guided score field. To address this issue, we propose an information-theoretic framework for CFG schedule optimization. Our approach uses a clean endpoint reference to specify the desired consistency-coverage trade-off, while optimizing the actual distribution induced by the guided sampler toward this reference. We derive trajectory-level formulas to estimate the objective from samples and score evaluations, avoiding explicit density estimation. On ImageNet-512 with EDM-XXL and COCO with SD-XL, the learned schedules achieve competitive or improved trade-offs over constant guidance and allocate guidance selectively across noise levels.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an information-theoretic framework to optimize classifier-free guidance (CFG) schedules across the reverse diffusion trajectory, addressing the consistency-coverage trade-off by learning noise-level-dependent guidance weights that improve upon constant guidance schedules.
AI.motivation: CFG improves condition consistency via guidance weight, but stronger guidance reduces diversity and distributional coverage. It's unclear how to control this trade-off across the reverse trajectory because the induced distribution differs from a simple fixed-time tilted distribution.
AI.method: An information-theoretic framework for CFG schedule optimization is proposed. It uses a clean endpoint reference to specify the desired consistency-coverage trade-off and optimizes the actual distribution induced by the guided sampler toward this reference. Trajectory-level formulas are derived to estimate the objective from samples and score evaluations, avoiding explicit density estimation.
AI.result: On ImageNet-512 with EDM-XXL and COCO with SD-XL, the learned schedules achieve competitive or improved trade-offs over constant guidance and allocate guidance selectively across noise levels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively optimizes CFG schedules, demonstrating that learned, noise-level-dependent guidance can outperform constant guidance in balancing condition consistency and distributional coverage.

title: RoPE-Aware Bit Allocation for KV-Cache Quantization
summary: Existing low-bit KV-cache quantizers often treat each cached key as a flat vector. Under RoPE, however, a key's contribution to a future attention logit decomposes into a position-dependent sum over two-dimensional frequency blocks. This makes key-cache quantization a block-wise bit-allocation problem: high-energy RoPE blocks are more sensitive to quantization error and should receive more bits. We introduce Block-GTQ, a RoPE-aware bit allocator for key-cache quantization built on TurboQuant-MSE(TQ-MSE). For each layer and KV head, Block-GTQ computes a label-free energy score for each RoPE block and greedily allocates integer bit widths by marginal gain. Under matched K/V bit budgets, Block-GTQ better preserves RoPE query-key logits on a ten-model diagnostic panel, cutting per-layer MAE by 32-80% at 2 and 3 b/dim K-only quantization and winning all 367/367 layer comparisons against uniform TQ-MSE. These fidelity gains translate to stronger downstream long-context retrieval, understanding, and reasoning. At K2V2 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Block-GTQ raises the six-task NIAH average from 70.6 to 97.4, and the LongBench-EN average from 36.87 to 53.31. On AIME 2024/2025 with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, without an fp16 recent-key buffer, Block-GTQ at K3V2 scores 51.7/37.5, close to fp16's 54.2/37.9, whereas uniform TQ-MSE collapses to 0.0/0.0. We further implement a packed-cache serving path. On a single H800 GPU with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, packed K3V3 achieves 3.24x KV-cache compression with fp16-comparable quality, runs 1.34x faster than fp16 FlashAttention2 at 128K context, reduces peak memory from 56.31 GB to 19.85 GB, and remains feasible at 256K and 512K where fp16 OOMs. Code is available at https://github.com/JIA-Lab-research/blockgtq.

AI.tldr: Block-GTQ is a RoPE-aware bit allocator for KV-cache quantization that assigns bits per frequency block rather than per flat vector, achieving significantly better fidelity and downstream performance under low-bit settings.
AI.motivation: Existing low-bit KV-cache quantizers treat each cached key as a flat vector, ignoring the block-wise structure of RoPE where keys contribute to attention logits via position-dependent sums over 2D frequency blocks. This oversight leads to suboptimal bit allocation.
AI.method: Block-GTQ computes a label-free energy score for each RoPE block per layer and KV head, then greedily allocates integer bit widths by marginal gain using TurboQuant-MSE (TQ-MSE) as the base quantizer.
AI.result: At matched bit budgets, Block-GTQ cuts per-layer MAE by 32-80% at 2 and 3 b/dim K-only quantization, winning all 367/367 layer comparisons. At K2V2 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, NIAH average rises from 70.6 to 97.4, LongBench-EN from 36.87 to 53.31. On AIME with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B at K3V2, scores reach 51.7/37.5 vs fp16's 54.2/37.9, while uniform TQ-MSE collapses to 0.0/0.0. Packed K3V3 on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct achieves 3.24x compression, 1.34x speedup at 128K, memory reduction from 56.31 GB to 19.85 GB, and works at 256K/512K where fp16 OOMs.
AI.conclusion: Block-GTQ demonstrates that RoPE-aware block-wise bit allocation is superior to flat vector quantization for KV-cache compression, providing large fidelity gains that translate into strong downstream improvements in long-context retrieval, understanding, and reasoning, while enabling practical deployment at extreme context lengths.

title: Rapid FinFET Modelling Using an Autoencoder
summary: This work presents a machine learning framework that leverages an autoencoder (AE) for the efficient modeling of FinFET. We first calibrated a BSIM-CMG model to generate a dataset of current-voltage (ID-VG) characteristics. This data was used to train an autoencoder that compresses full I-V curves into a low-dimensional latent space, which intrinsically encodes key device physics. A key innovation is the explicit incorporation of parameter such as drain to source voltage (VDS) as an input feature, enhancing the model ability to capture bias dependent variation. The trained model successfully reconstructs full I-V curves and directly extracts critical device metrics including threshold voltage (VTH), subthreshold slope (SS), and peak transconductance (gm). This approach demonstrates that data driven compact models, built from actual characterization data, can achieve high accuracy with minimal training data, providing a powerful tool for rapid device characterization, modelling and circuit level simulation.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework using an autoencoder for efficient FinFET modeling, trained on BSIM-CMG data, that compresses I-V curves into latent space and extracts key device metrics with high accuracy from minimal data.
AI.motivation: To develop a data-driven compact model for FinFET devices that can achieve high accuracy with minimal training data, enabling rapid device characterization and circuit-level simulation.
AI.method: An autoencoder (AE) is trained on I-V curve data generated from a calibrated BSIM-CMG model. The AE compresses full I-V curves into a low-dimensional latent space. Drain-to-source voltage (VDS) is explicitly incorporated as an input feature to capture bias-dependent variation.
AI.result: The autoencoder successfully reconstructs full I-V curves and directly extracts critical device metrics: threshold voltage (VTH), subthreshold slope (SS), and peak transconductance (gm), achieving high accuracy with minimal training data.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven compact models built from actual characterization data can provide a powerful tool for rapid device characterization, modeling, and circuit-level simulation.

title: RAVEN: A Regime-Aware Variable-context Expert Network for Financial Time Series Forecasting
summary: Financial time series forecasting presents structural challenges absent from standard benchmarks. Log-returns are non-stationary, exhibit exceptionally low signal-to-noise (SNR) ratios, and are governed by regime-dependent temporal dependencies. We identify a key limitation of state-of-the-art (SOTA) time series models in financial settings. A fixed context window is mismatched to the time-varying optimal look-back of non-stationary price processes. We propose the Regime-Aware Variable-context Expert Network (RAVEN), a Mixture-of-Experts framework designed to adaptively determine the temporal context for each input sample. Instead of relying on a fixed look-back horizon, RAVEN constructs a hierarchy of nested contiguous windows whose lengths are determined by the data itself. Specifically, RAVEN scores patches by learned importance in reverse chronological order and applies the Cumulative Importance Thresholding (CIT) mechanism to derive nested prefix windows, each routed to a scale-specialized expert. A Global Compressed Representation (GCR) branch runs in parallel over the full context, preserving global temporal coherence that local experts cannot guarantee. Because the nested routing induces structured overlap among expert inputs, we introduce a Correlation-Aware Weighting (CAW) to align variable-length expert outputs and penalize pairwise cosine similarity prior to aggregation. Experiments on cumulative log-return prediction (HS300, S&P500) and fund sales forecasting demonstrate that RAVEN achieves SOTA performances, improves Pearson correlation by 9.2% on HS300 and 20.2% on S&P500, and reduces MSE by 18.2% on fund sales forecasting, while achieving the best results in 14 of 16 metrics on four PEMS traffic benchmarks.

AI.tldr: RAVEN is a Mixture-of-Experts framework that adaptively determines temporal context windows via Cumulative Importance Thresholding and Correlation-Aware Weighting, achieving SOTA results on financial time series (HS300, S&P500, fund sales) and traffic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Financial time series suffer from non-stationarity, low SNR, and regime-dependent temporal dependencies. Fixed context windows in existing models mismatched to the time-varying optimal look-back for non-stationary price processes.
AI.method: RAVEN constructs a hierarchy of nested contiguous windows via learned importance scoring and Cumulative Importance Thresholding (CIT) to derive scale-adaptive prefixes. Each prefix is routed to a scale-specialized expert, while a Global Compressed Representation (GCR) branch preserves global coherence. Correlation-Aware Weighting (CAW) handles structured overlap among expert outputs.
AI.result: Improves Pearson correlation by 9.2% on HS300 and 20.2% on S&P500; reduces MSE by 18.2% on fund sales forecasting; achieves best results in 14 of 16 metrics on four PEMS traffic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive variable-context mechanisms significantly outperform fixed-window approaches for non-stationary financial time series, demonstrating cross-domain effectiveness on traffic forecasting as well.

title: Blockwise Policy-Drift Gating for On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student policy using teacher signals computed on trajectories sampled by the student itself. Recent work shows that sampled-token OPD can be fragile on long-horizon reasoning tasks and that local teacher-support matching is a simple and effective repair. This paper introduces blockwise policy-drift gating, a lightweight student-only old-current drift controller for OPD under rollout reuse. The method computes log-probability shifts between the behavior student and the current student on the sampled token path, aggregates these shifts over fixed blocks or spans, and uses the resulting detached, mean-normalized gates to reweight OPD position losses. It does not change teacher targets, teacher top-K supports, or the rollout policy. In a six-variant Qwen3 math reasoning benchmark with a uniform 200-step training budget for all trained variants, we use pass@8 as the primary problem-level solve-rate metric. Fixed 64-token block gating improves sampled-token OPD mean pass@8 from 0.4978 to 0.5160 across AIME24, AIME25, MATH500, and AMC23. On Teacher-TopK/LSM, Block64 gives the best four-benchmark mean pass@8 among trained students. The results identify local old-current policy drift as a practical control signal for reused OPD rollouts and motivate block-level gating as a simple default for improving solve-rate robustness.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces blockwise policy-drift gating, a lightweight method that uses log-probability shifts between behavior and current student policies over fixed token blocks to reweight OPD losses, improving pass@8 from 0.4978 to 0.5160 on math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Sampled-token on-policy distillation (OPD) is fragile on long-horizon reasoning tasks, and existing fixes like local teacher-support matching are effective but still leave room for improvement in rollout reuse settings.
AI.method: Blockwise policy-drift gating computes log-probability shifts between behavior student and current student on sampled token paths, aggregates them over fixed blocks (e.g., 64 tokens), and uses detached mean-normalized gates to reweight OPD position losses, without altering teacher targets, top-K supports, or rollout policy.
AI.result: Fixed 64-token block gating improves sampled-token OPD mean pass@8 from 0.4978 to 0.5160 across AIME24, AIME25, MATH500, and AMC23. Block64 achieves the best four-benchmark mean pass@8 among all trained students on Teacher-TopK/LSM.
AI.conclusion: Local old-current policy drift serves as a practical control signal for reused OPD rollouts, and block-level gating is a simple default for improving solve-rate robustness in on-policy distillation.

title: NeuroSonic: Conditional Flow Matching for EEG-to-Speech Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing continuous speech from scalp electroencephalography (EEG) remains fundamentally challenging. EEG provides a weak, spatially diffuse, and highly variable measurement of distributed cortical activity, whereas speech is organized as a coherent acoustic trajectory with strong harmonic and temporal structure. The resulting mismatch makes waveform regression unstable and causes stochastic multi-step generation to be sensitive to artifact-dependent conditioning and subject variability. We introduce NeuroSonic, a conditional flow-matching framework for EEG-to-speech reconstruction. Instead of predicting waveforms directly or refining them through stochastic denoising, NeuroSonic learns a deterministic probability-flow velocity field that transports a noise-corrupted acoustic state toward clean speech under EEG conditioning. EEG and audio are embedded into a shared token space and processed by a time-conditioned gated Transformer that parameterizes the transport ordinary differential equation. This formulation models trajectory evolution explicitly while avoiding iterative stochastic sampling. We evaluate NeuroSonic on the CineBrain and EAV benchmarks under cross-subject evaluation. Across both datasets, the proposed method improves distributional realism, spectral fidelity, and perceptual quality over representative GAN-, diffusion-, and mean-flow baselines, with up to a 26.3\% gain in overall perceptual quality. The performance gap is most evident in artifact-heavy segments, where conditioning variability is strongest. These findings indicate that deterministic conditional transport provides a stable and effective formulation for EEG-driven speech reconstruction. Code is available at https://github.com/Y-Research-SBU/NeuroSonic/ .

AI.tldr: NeuroSonic is a conditional flow-matching framework for EEG-to-speech reconstruction that learns a deterministic probability-flow velocity field to transport noise-corrupted acoustic states toward clean speech under EEG conditioning, achieving up to 26.3% improvement in perceptual quality over GAN, diffusion, and mean-flow baselines on CineBrain and EAV benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing continuous speech from scalp EEG remains challenging due to the mismatch between weak, spatially diffuse, variable EEG measurements and coherent acoustic speech structure, causing unstable waveform regression and artifact-sensitive multi-step generation.
AI.method: NeuroSonic uses a conditional flow-matching framework: EEG and audio are embedded into a shared token space, processed by a time-conditioned gated Transformer that parameterizes a transport ODE. Instead of stochastic denoising, it learns a deterministic probability-flow velocity field to transport noise-corrupted acoustic states toward clean speech conditioned on EEG.
AI.result: On CineBrain and EAV datasets under cross-subject evaluation, NeuroSonic improves distributional realism, spectral fidelity, and perceptual quality over GAN, diffusion, and mean-flow baselines, with up to 26.3% gain in overall perceptual quality, especially in artifact-heavy segments.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic conditional transport provides a stable and effective formulation for EEG-driven speech reconstruction, avoiding the instability of direct waveform regression and iterative stochastic sampling.

title: FedUP: One-Shot Federated Unlearning via Centroid-Guided Plug-in Filters
summary: Federated unlearning (FU) is critical for complying with legal mandates like the right to be forgotten in decentralized systems, yet current methods face a persistent dilemma between non-target knowledge loss and high request latency. To resolve these issues, we propose FedUP, a one-shot federated unlearning framework utilizing lightweight pluggable filters that act as a "knowledge funnel" to screen out target data while preserving original model performance. By freezing original model parameters and training filters at the server side using differentially private (DP)-protected class centroid samples, FedUP bypasses the need for multi-round client-server communication and complex retraining, reducing unlearning latency from minutes to mere seconds. Additionally, the framework's pluggable architecture ensures inherent reversibility, enabling the seamless restoration of forgotten knowledge by simply removing the filters. Extensive experiments on diverse image and text tasks demonstrate that FedUP effectively reduces non-target knowledge loss and achieves superior unlearning precision and efficiency across various scenarios. Code is available at: https://github.com/suows/FedUP-code.

AI.tldr: FedUP is a one-shot federated unlearning framework that uses lightweight pluggable filters (as a "knowledge funnel") to remove target data while preserving model performance, reducing unlearning latency from minutes to seconds without multi-round communication or complex retraining.
AI.motivation: Current federated unlearning methods suffer from a trade-off between non-target knowledge loss and high request latency, making them inefficient for complying with legal mandates like the right to be forgotten.
AI.method: FedUP freezes original model parameters and trains pluggable filters at the server side using DP-protected class centroid samples, enabling one-shot unlearning without multi-round client-server communication.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse image and text tasks show FedUP effectively reduces non-target knowledge loss and achieves superior unlearning precision and efficiency; unlearning latency drops from minutes to seconds.
AI.conclusion: FedUP resolves the dilemma between non-target knowledge loss and high latency in federated unlearning, while its pluggable architecture provides inherent reversibility for seamless knowledge restoration.

title: When Top-1 Fails: Calibrating LoRA Monitors for Masked Diffusion LMs
comment: 14 pages, 3 figures. Code and result artifacts: https://github.com/lucky-verma/top1-fails-dlm-lora-monitors
summary: Discrete diffusion language model (DLM) fine-tuning inherits inexpensive diagnostics from denoising-time confidence monitors, but their PEFT-training meaning is untested. We test top-1 argmax concentration as a collapse warning. Across 816 LoRA/PEFT configurations from three DLM families, the warning fires for every configuration while logs record 0/816 actual collapses at the 200 step horizon, giving zero precision. The cause is pre-equilibrium saturation: top-1 concentration is already high before optimization and quickly becomes insensitive to final training stability. We then evaluate max LoRA gradient norm, a parameter-side signal that samples gradient routing rather than token concentration. On a pooled held-out LLaDA-family split, a train-optimized threshold identifies top-decile final-loss configurations with precision 0.68 and F1=0.79, above the all-positive top-1 baseline even at the lower split-bootstrap confidence bound. Autoregressive controls and cross-family threshold failures bound the result to short-horizon DLM-LoRA inspection rather than a universal collapse detector. Workflow: drop top-1 as a PEFT alarm, log max-gradient early in training, and calibrate thresholds per DLM family before routing runs for inspection.

AI.tldr: Top-1 argmax concentration fails as a collapse warning in DLM LoRA fine-tuning (zero precision across 816 configurations due to pre-equilibrium saturation), while max LoRA gradient norm achieves precision 0.68 and F1=0.79 for identifying top-decile bad configurations in LLaDA-family models, though thresholds are family-specific and not universally transferable.
AI.motivation: To test whether top-1 argmax concentration, a confidence monitor inherited from denoising-time diagnostics in discrete diffusion language models, remains a valid collapse warning signal when applied to PEFT (LoRA) fine-tuning scenarios.
AI.method: Evaluated top-1 argmax concentration across 816 LoRA/PEFT configurations from three DLM families; proposed max LoRA gradient norm as an alternative parameter-side signal that samples gradient routing; trained threshold on pooled LLaDA-family held-out split to identify top-decile final-loss configurations.
AI.result: Top-1 concentration fires for all 816 configurations with 0/816 actual collapses (zero precision). Max LoRA gradient norm achieves precision 0.68 and F1=0.79 on LLaDA-family split, surpassing the all-positive top-1 baseline. Autoregressive controls and cross-family threshold failures confirm results are limited to short-horizon DLM-LoRA inspection.
AI.conclusion: Drop top-1 argmax concentration as a PEFT collapse alarm; instead log max-gradient early in training and calibrate thresholds per DLM family before routing runs for inspection.

title: Holistic Data Scheduler for LLM Pre-training via Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
summary: The composition of training data, governed by the diversity of sources and their mixing strategy, is a cornerstone of Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training. Online Data Mixing (ODM), the technique of adaptively adjusting data mixtures during training, has emerged as a promising direction to improve efficiency. However, existing methods are constrained by their reliance on a singular optimization perspective, which fundamentally overlooks the need for complex LLM pre-training to consider the dynamic data composition from multiple dimensions. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS), a novel online data mixing framework. HDS formulates the data scheduling challenge as a reinforcement learning problem in a continuous control space and leverages the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm for its stability and sample efficiency in exploring the high-dimensional policy space. At the core of HDS lies a novel multi-objective, holistic reward function that integrates three critical perspectives: a data-driven reward for quality, a loss-driven reward capturing inter-domain influence, and a model-driven reward based on weight norms. To validate our design and determine its optimal configuration, we conducted systematic experiments on LLMs of various sizes. On The Pile benchmark, HDS reaches the final validation perplexity of the next best method with 44% fewer training iterations. Furthermore, it achieves a 7.2% improvement on the MMLU 0-shot task along with consistent gains on other benchmarks, showcasing its ability to enhance both training efficiency and final model capability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS), an online data mixing framework for LLM pre-training that uses reinforcement learning (Soft Actor-Critic) with a multi-objective reward function to dynamically adjust data mixtures, achieving 44% faster convergence and 7.2% improvement on MMLU 0-shot.
AI.motivation: Existing Online Data Mixing (ODM) methods are constrained by relying on a singular optimization perspective, failing to account for the multi-dimensional nature of dynamic data composition needed for complex LLM pre-training.
AI.method: HDS formulates data scheduling as a continuous-control RL problem using Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), with a holistic reward function integrating three perspectives: (1) data-driven reward for quality, (2) loss-driven reward capturing inter-domain influence, and (3) model-driven reward based on weight norms.
AI.result: On The Pile benchmark, HDS reaches the validation perplexity of the next best method with 44% fewer training iterations, and achieves a 7.2% improvement on MMLU 0-shot with consistent gains on other benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HDS demonstrates that multi-objective optimization via RL-based online data mixing significantly improves both training efficiency and final LLM capability compared to prior single-objective ODM methods.

title: A Time-Reparameterized Cumulative Intensity Extrapolation Sampler for Discrete Flow Matching
summary: Discrete flow matching (DFM) provides a principled framework for generative modeling on discrete state spaces via continuous-time Markov chain dynamics. In practice, sampling for DFM commonly employs discretizations such as $τ$-leaping, yet efficient sampling methods under a limited number of function evaluations (NFE) remain less studied. To address this gap, we propose the Time-Reparameterized Cumulative Intensity Extrapolation (TR-CIE) sampler, which aims to improve sampling quality when function evaluations are restricted. TR-CIE consists of two components. First, a schedule-based time reparameterization rescales the time grid according to the noise schedule. Under standard factorized DFM rate parameterizations, this transformation of variables absorbs the schedule-dependent growth term and mitigates stiffness near the terminal sampling stage. Second, we introduce a cumulative-intensity extrapolation updating rule. By reusing cached model outputs from the previous step as a history term, this improves the approximation of stepwise cumulative intensities on the resulting non-uniform time grid. We provide a theoretical analysis that bounds the local approximation error of cumulative intensities and establishes convergence results. The resulting sampler requires one NFE per step and introduces no additional model evaluations compared to the standard $τ$-leaping sampler. Extensive experiments on synthetic tasks, text generation, and text-to-image benchmarks demonstrate that our method improves sampling quality under limited NFE.

AI.tldr: Proposes TR-CIE sampler for discrete flow matching, combining time reparameterization and cumulative intensity extrapolation to improve sampling quality under limited function evaluations.
AI.motivation: Discrete flow matching (DFM) sampling commonly uses τ-leaping discretization, but efficient sampling methods under a limited number of function evaluations (NFE) are understudied.
AI.method: TR-CIE sampler with two components: (1) schedule-based time reparameterization that rescales the time grid to mitigate stiffness near terminal sampling, and (2) cumulative-intensity extrapolation updating rule that reuses cached model outputs from previous step to improve approximation of stepwise cumulative intensities.
AI.result: Requires one NFE per step with no additional model evaluations vs. standard τ-leaping; theoretical bounds on local approximation error and convergence results; demonstrated improvement on synthetic tasks, text generation, and text-to-image benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TR-CIE effectively improves sampling quality under limited NFE for discrete flow matching models.

title: AsyncOPD: How Stale Can On-Policy Distillation Be?
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own rollouts guided by teacher feedback and is becoming increasingly important for large language model (LLM) post-training. Like reinforcement learning (RL), however, OPD faces an on-policy systems bottleneck, as rollouts can dominate training time for reasoning workloads. Asynchronous training pipelines can alleviate this bottleneck by decoupling rollout generation from learner updates, but doing so introduces stale-policy data. While prior work has studied stale data in asynchronous RL, its effects in OPD remain underexplored. We present the first systematic study of staleness in asynchronous OPD, focusing on a practical setting where teacher feedback is implemented through local KL losses and full-vocabulary teacher logits are too expensive to store or transfer, necessitating finite teacher-score caches. We first show that KL direction changes the stale-data problem: teacher-weighted forward KL is more robust to stale rollouts, whereas student-weighted reverse KL is vulnerable. Second, for this vulnerable reverse-KL case, we study whether methods designed to stabilize asynchronous RL can mitigate OPD staleness. In our experiments, they do not improve over a simpler OPD-specific surrogate: recomputing the reverse-KL signal under the current student at learner time. Third, we analyze how finite teacher-score caches create a bias-variance tradeoff for sparse and sampled reverse-KL OPD estimators. This motivates multi-sample Monte Carlo (MC), which preserves MC correctability while reducing one-sample variance. Finally, we present and open-source AsyncOPD, a fully asynchronous OPD training pipeline built from these estimator choices. Experiments show that AsyncOPD improves training throughput by $1.6\times$ to $3.8\times$ over strict synchronous training while reaching comparable accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies staleness in asynchronous on-policy distillation (OPD) for LLM post-training, showing that forward KL is robust to stale rollouts while reverse KL is vulnerable, and that existing asynchronous RL methods don't help; instead, recomputing reverse-KL signals and using multi-sample Monte Carlo estimators improve performance. The proposed AsyncOPD pipeline achieves 1.6×–3.8× throughput speedup over synchronous training with comparable accuracy.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) for LLM post-training suffers from an on-policy systems bottleneck where rollouts dominate training time, similar to RL. Asynchronous training can alleviate this but introduces stale-policy data, and its effects in OPD are underexplored.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic study of staleness in asynchronous OPD under a practical setting with local KL losses and finite teacher-score caches. They compare forward vs. reverse KL directions, test asynchronous RL stabilization methods, propose recomputing reverse-KL signals under the current student, and analyze bias-variance tradeoffs with multi-sample Monte Carlo estimators, culminating in the AsyncOPD pipeline.
AI.result: Forward KL is robust to stale rollouts; reverse KL is vulnerable. Asynchronous RL methods do not improve over recomputing reverse-KL signals at learner time. Multi-sample MC reduces variance for sparse/sampled reverse-KL estimators. AsyncOPD achieves 1.6×–3.8× throughput improvement with comparable accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first systematic study of staleness in asynchronous OPD, identifies key design choices for robust asynchronous training, and demonstrates that AsyncOPD can significantly improve training throughput without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Lightweight Transformer Models for On-Device Fault Detection: A Benchmark Study on Resource-Constrained Deployment
summary: On-device fault detection enables real-time diagnostics without cloud dependency, but deploying machine learning models on resource-constrained hardware demands careful tradeoffs between accuracy, latency, and model size. We present a benchmark comparing traditional ML methods (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, Logistic Regression) against lightweight transformer architectures (DistilBERT, TinyBERT-6L, TinyBERT-4L, MobileBERT) for binary fault detection across three public datasets: NASA C-MAPSS turbofan degradation, SECOM semiconductor manufacturing, and UCI AI4I 2020 predictive maintenance. We evaluate classification performance (F1-score, AUC), model size, and CPU inference latency, and further assess INT8 dynamic quantization and a two-stage adaptive inference pipeline. Our results reveal that on well-separated sensor data (C-MAPSS), lightweight transformers match traditional ML at 87.8% F1 but at 100x the model size and 9000x the latency. TinyBERT-4L emerges as the most deployment-friendly transformer at 55 MB and 18 ms CPU latency. INT8 quantization reduces size by 25% while preserving 86.9% F1. Our adaptive pipeline, routing 97.9% of predictions through a quantized triage model and only 2.1% to a larger expert, achieves 87.6% F1 at 19.5 ms average latency. On severely imbalanced datasets (SECOM, UCI-PM), both traditional and transformer methods struggle significantly, highlighting fundamental limitations of current approaches for extreme class imbalance in fault detection. All code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks traditional ML (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, Logistic Regression) against lightweight transformers (DistilBERT, TinyBERT variants, MobileBERT) for on-device binary fault detection on three public datasets. Key findings: on well-separated data (C-MAPSS), transformers match traditional ML (87.8% F1) but at 100x size and 9000x latency; TinyBERT-4L is the most efficient transformer (55MB, 18ms latency); INT8 quantization reduces size 25% with minimal F1 loss; an adaptive pipeline achieves 87.6% F1 at 19.5ms latency by routing 97.9% of predictions through a quantized triage model. Both approaches struggle severely on imbalanced datasets.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time on-device fault detection without cloud dependency by systematically evaluating tradeoffs between accuracy, latency, and model size across traditional ML and lightweight transformer methods on resource-constrained hardware.
AI.method: Benchmarks four traditional ML methods and four lightweight transformers across three public fault detection datasets. Evaluates F1-score, AUC, model size, and CPU inference latency, plus INT8 dynamic quantization and a two-stage adaptive inference pipeline.
AI.result: Lightweight transformers match traditional ML on well-separated data but at 100x size and 9000x latency. TinyBERT-4L is the most deployment-friendly transformer (55MB, 18ms). INT8 quantization reduces size 25% with 86.9% F1. Adaptive pipeline achieves 87.6% F1 at 19.5ms latency. Both approaches fail on severely imbalanced datasets.
AI.conclusion: For on-device fault detection with balanced data, traditional ML offers superior efficiency; transformers only viable with aggressive optimization (quantization, adaptive inference). Extreme class imbalance remains a fundamental limitation for both paradigms.

title: Project Ariadne: Prompt-Conditioned Route Generation for Synthesis Planning
summary: Retrosynthetic planning seeks to connect a target molecule to commercially available starting materials through a multistep route. Classical planners construct such routes by iteratively applying single-step reaction models within a search procedure; constrained variants often require specialized algorithms or architectural changes. Direct route generation reframes retrosynthesis as sequence generation, but existing direct-generation methods still train separate models for different planning specifications. We introduce Ariadne, a decoder-only route generator that represents the target, optional constraints, and route in one prompt-completion sequence. On the RetroCast/PaRoutes mkt-cnv-160 benchmark family, one 24-layer checkpoint follows route-depth and required-starting-material prompts: adding the corresponding prompt fields raises Solv-0 by 13.7 points for depth constraints and 31.2 points for required-leaf constraints. Ariadne also improves over DESP, a bidirectional search planner, on required-leaf Top-10 and Solv-0 in 24 GPU-minutes versus 6.8 GPU-hours. On standard reconstruction, Ariadne is comparable to DMS Explorer XL at about half the reported inference time. Across additional target-only benchmarks, Ariadne's clearest gains are on route-holdout reconstruction, whereas AiZynthFinder MCTS remains stronger on several Solv-0 comparisons. These results extend sequence generation from specialist retrosynthesis models to prompt-conditioned structural route generation. We release the codebase and training scripts to support further work, but do not introduce Tier-1--3 route checkers; those remain the main bottleneck before models of this kind can become useful to experimental chemists.

AI.tldr: Ariadne is a decoder-only retrosynthetic route generator that uses prompt-conditioned sequence generation to handle constrained planning (depth and required starting materials) in a single model, achieving competitive or superior performance against search-based planners like DESP and AiZynthFinder while using significantly less compute.
AI.motivation: Existing retrosynthetic planners either rely on iterative search procedures with single-step models (which require specialized algorithms for constraints) or direct route generation methods that train separate models for each planning specification. The authors aim to unify these approaches with a single model that handles multiple constraint types via prompt conditioning.
AI.method: Ariadne is a decoder-only transformer that frames retrosynthesis as prompt-completion sequence generation, where the target molecule, optional constraints (route depth, required starting materials), and the full route are represented in a single sequence. It uses a 24-layer checkpoint and is trained on the RetroCast/PaRoutes mkt-cnv-160 benchmark.
AI.result: On constrained planning, adding prompt fields raises Solv-0 by 13.7 points (depth) and 31.2 points (required-leaf). Ariadne outperforms DESP on required-leaf Top-10 and Solv-0 using 24 GPU-minutes vs. 6.8 GPU-hours. On standard reconstruction, it matches DMS Explorer XL at half the inference time. On target-only benchmarks, it excels at route-holdout reconstruction, though AiZynthFinder MCTS remains stronger on some Solv-0 metrics.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-conditioned sequence generation can effectively replace specialized search algorithms for constrained retrosynthetic planning, but Tier-1–3 route checkers remain the main bottleneck before such models become practically useful for experimental chemists.

title: Managing Task Execution for Unknown Workloads in Batteryless IoT: A Hardware-Agnostic Evaluation
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Computing
summary: In recent years, the Internet of Things (IoT) paradigm has been shifting toward batteryless, energy-harvesting architectures. Sustaining reliable operation in these systems requires intelligent management of highly volatile stored energy. As edge applications grow in complexity, traditional energy-aware schedulers struggle with unpredictable workloads due to their reliance on static execution thresholds or pre-measured, hardware-specific task profiles. To overcome this, we propose two novel, hardware-agnostic dynamic scheduling strategies treating applications as a "black box," requiring no prior energy information: a model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent and an on-the-fly Approximated Prediction (AP) method. We evaluate these methods against an adaptive task rate approach (AsTAR) and optimized static thresholds using a custom-built, physically accurate simulation framework driven by real-world solar data and dynamic LoRa transmission profiles. Rather than claiming universal superiority, our analysis exposes the distinct operational trade-offs of each method: the AP approach delivers lightweight, near-oracle task throughput; the RL agent provides tunable survival-execution balancing; and AsTAR excels at execution pacing across long energy gaps. Finally, we demonstrate that while these advanced strategies provide critical resilience for severely constrained systems with small capacitors, devices with larger energy buffers can efficiently rely on simpler, less computationally expensive static policies.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two hardware-agnostic dynamic scheduling strategies (RL-based and Approximated Prediction) for batteryless IoT devices and evaluates them against existing methods, revealing distinct trade-offs in operational performance depending on energy buffer size and workload characteristics.
AI.motivation: Traditional energy-aware schedulers for batteryless IoT devices rely on static execution thresholds or pre-measured hardware-specific profiles, which struggle with unpredictable workloads as edge applications grow in complexity.
AI.method: Two hardware-agnostic dynamic scheduling strategies are proposed: a model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent and an on-the-fly Approximated Prediction (AP) method, treating applications as a "black box." They are evaluated against AsTAR (adaptive task rate) and optimized static thresholds using a custom simulation with real-world solar data and dynamic LoRa transmission profiles.
AI.result: AP provides lightweight, near-oracle task throughput; RL offers tunable survival-execution balancing; AsTAR excels at execution pacing across long energy gaps. Advanced strategies are critical for small-capacitor systems, while larger energy buffers can rely on simpler static policies.
AI.conclusion: There is no universally superior scheduler; the choice depends on energy buffer size and operational requirements. Hardware-agnostic dynamic strategies provide resilience for severely constrained devices, but simpler static approaches suffice for devices with larger energy buffers.

title: Parallel Manifold Steering: Efficient Adaptation of Large Associative Memories via Residual Energy Shaping
summary: Large Transformer models function as Dense Associative Memories (DAMs), retrieving knowledge via high-dimensional attractor dynamics driven by the self-attention mechanism \citep{ramsauer2020hopfield, wu2024attention}. However, adapting these frozen memory systems to new tasks presents a fundamental ``Plasticity-Stability'' dilemma. Current methods either risk catastrophic interference by modifying synaptic weights directly (e.g., LoRA) \citep{hu2021lora} or degrade associative capacity by clogging the retrieval buffer with static prompt tokens (e.g., VPT) \citep{jia2022vpt}. In this work, we propose \textbf{H-Res} (Hierarchical Residual Steering), a mechanism that modulates the effective energy landscape of the Transformer without altering its global equilibrium or expanding its sequence length. By formulating adaptation as a control problem on the activation manifold \citep{chen2018neuralode}, H-Res learns a state-dependent vector field that steers token trajectories into task-specific basins of attraction. We formally prove that H-Res preserves the attention entropy of the foundation model and facilitates Neural Collapse \citep{papyan2020prevalence}. Empirically, Manifold Steering outperforms global weight modification by 26\% on associative retrieval tasks and eliminates the computational overhead of prompt-based methods, scaling effectively to structured domains \citep{zha2023vtab}.

AI.tldr: H-Res (Hierarchical Residual Steering) is a method that adapts frozen Transformer models to new tasks by learning a state-dependent vector field that modulates the attention energy landscape without modifying weights or adding prompt tokens, preserving associative memory capacity while enabling task-specific retrieval.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptation methods for frozen Transformer models face a Plasticity-Stability dilemma: direct weight modification (e.g., LoRA) causes catastrophic interference, while prompt-based methods (e.g., VPT) degrade associative capacity by clogging the retrieval buffer with static tokens.
AI.method: H-Res formulates adaptation as a control problem on the activation manifold, learning a state-dependent vector field that steers token trajectories into task-specific basins of attraction without altering the model's global equilibrium or expanding sequence length.
AI.result: Manifold Steering outperforms global weight modification by 26% on associative retrieval tasks and eliminates computational overhead of prompt-based methods, scaling effectively to structured domains.
AI.conclusion: H-Res preserves attention entropy of the foundation model and facilitates Neural Collapse, offering a principled solution to the plasticity-stability trade-off in adapting frozen Transformers viewed as Dense Associative Memories.

title: Natural Identifiers for Privacy and Data Audits in Large Language Models
summary: Assessing the privacy of large language models (LLMs) presents significant challenges. In particular, most existing methods for auditing differential privacy require the insertion of specially crafted canary data during training, making them impractical for auditing already-trained models without costly retraining. Additionally, dataset inference, which audits whether a suspect dataset was used to train a model, is infeasible without access to a private non-member held-out dataset. Yet, such held-out datasets are often unavailable or difficult to construct for real-world cases since they have to be from the same distribution (IID) as the suspect data. These limitations severely hinder the ability to conduct scalable, post-hoc audits. To enable such audits, this work introduces natural identifiers (NIDs) as a novel solution to the above-mentioned challenges. NIDs are structured random strings, such as cryptographic hashes and shortened URLs, naturally occurring in common LLM training datasets. Their format enables the generation of unlimited additional random strings from the same distribution, which can act as alternative canaries for audits and as same-distribution held-out data for dataset inference. Our evaluation highlights that indeed, using NIDs, we can facilitate post-hoc differential privacy auditing without any retraining and enable dataset inference for any suspect dataset containing NIDs without the need for a private non-member held-out dataset.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces natural identifiers (NIDs)—structured random strings like cryptographic hashes and shortened URLs that naturally occur in training data—to enable post-hoc differential privacy auditing and dataset inference for LLMs without requiring retraining with canaries or access to private held-out datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for auditing LLM privacy either require inserting canary data during training (impractical for already-trained models) or rely on unavailable private held-out datasets for dataset inference. These limitations hinder scalable, post-hoc privacy audits.
AI.method: The authors propose using natural identifiers (NIDs)—structured random strings such as cryptographic hashes and shortened URLs that already exist in common LLM training datasets. Because NIDs have a known format, unlimited additional random strings from the same distribution can be generated, serving as synthetic canaries for differential privacy auditing and as same-distribution held-out data for dataset inference.
AI.result: Using NIDs, the authors demonstrate that post-hoc differential privacy auditing is feasible without retraining, and dataset inference can be performed for any suspect dataset containing NIDs without requiring a private non-member held-out dataset.
AI.conclusion: NIDs provide a practical solution to privacy auditing challenges, enabling scalable post-hoc audits of LLMs without costly retraining or reliance on unavailable held-out datasets.

title: Data Augmentation: A Fourier Analysis Perspective
summary: Data augmentation is a simple and model-agnostic approach for exploiting known invariances in learning problems. Given a group acting on the input space, one augments the training set with transformed copies of each sample. Because it exploits symmetries without modifying the underlying learning algorithm, data augmentation can be applied broadly across learning methods. However, this universality comes at a computational cost: when the group is large, full group-sized augmentation quickly becomes computationally infeasible. This raises a fundamental question: Can partial data augmentation achieve the same statistical benefits as full augmentation in terms of generalization and sample complexity? We develop a general framework for investigating this question using Fourier analysis and the representation theory of finite groups. We show that, for a broad class of classical learning problems, partial data augmentation based on a randomly sampled subset of group elements achieves the same minimax rates as full augmentation, up to an approximation error that vanishes as the subset size increases. Our results provide a theoretical explanation for why partial augmentation can retain the statistical benefits of full augmentation despite enforcing symmetry only approximately, and shed light on a recently raised question in learning with symmetries: whether statistically optimal learning under general group invariances can be achieved using computationally scalable methods. Moreover, we prove a complementary impossibility result: enforcing exact invariance via data augmentation requires averaging over the entire group, and cannot be achieved by any strict subset when the hypothesis space is sufficiently expressive. Together, these results provide a unified perspective on full and partial data augmentation, as well as exact and approximate symmetry enforcement.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a Fourier-analytic framework to show that partial data augmentation (using a random subset of group elements) can achieve the same minimax rates as full group-sized augmentation, with vanishing approximation error as subset size grows. However, exact invariance requires full group averaging and cannot be achieved by any strict subset when the hypothesis space is sufficiently expressive.
AI.motivation: Data augmentation is widely used to exploit known invariances, but full group-sized augmentation is computationally infeasible for large groups. The key question is whether partial augmentation can match the statistical benefits (generalization and sample complexity) of full augmentation.
AI.method: The authors use Fourier analysis and representation theory of finite groups to build a general theoretical framework for comparing full and partial data augmentation in terms of minimax rates and approximation errors.
AI.result: Partial augmentation with random subset sampling achieves the same minimax rates as full augmentation, with approximation error decaying to zero as subset size increases. Conversely, exact invariance provably requires averaging over the entire group when the hypothesis space is expressive enough.
AI.conclusion: Partial data augmentation provides a computationally scalable approach that retains the statistical benefits of full augmentation, offering a unified perspective on exact vs. approximate symmetry enforcement in learning.

title: An LLM-based Two-Stage Transformer Framework for Cross-Domain Bearing Fault Diagnosis with Limited Data
comment: Accepted as a conference article of AIM 2026
summary: Bearing fault diagnosis faces critical challenges when dataset heterogeneity, operating condition variations, and limited labeled data occur simultaneously in industrial environments. Existing approaches address these issues in isolation and rely on implicit feature alignment, limiting effectiveness under concurrent challenges. This paper proposes a knowledge-guided two-stage transfer learning framework that employs a lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer with causal self-attention for hierarchical feature extraction from vibration signals, establishing explicit pathways where pre-trained encoder weights and fault prototype embeddings serve as knowledge carriers from multi-source pre-training to target adaptation. The framework addresses the dual-shift challenge through multi-source learning for generalizable representations, prototype-based knowledge modulation for target adaptation, and taxonomy-adaptive classification for seamless transfer across heterogeneous fault categories. Experimental validation on four real-world datasets demonstrates 92.61% average accuracy with only 10% labeled target data, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 17.24 percentage points, establishing a practical pathway toward cost-effective predictive maintenance in Industry 4.0 applications.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-guided two-stage transfer learning framework using lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer for bearing fault diagnosis achieves 92.61% accuracy with only 10% labeled target data, outperforming SOTA by 17.24 percentage points under concurrent dataset heterogeneity, operating condition variations, and limited labels.
AI.motivation: Bearing fault diagnosis faces critical challenges when dataset heterogeneity, operating condition variations, and limited labeled data occur simultaneously in industrial environments. Existing approaches address these issues in isolation and rely on implicit feature alignment, limiting effectiveness under concurrent challenges.
AI.method: A knowledge-guided two-stage transfer learning framework using a lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer with causal self-attention for hierarchical feature extraction from vibration signals. It establishes explicit pathways where pre-trained encoder weights and fault prototype embeddings serve as knowledge carriers. Addresses dual-shift challenge via multi-source learning, prototype-based knowledge modulation, and taxonomy-adaptive classification.
AI.result: 92.61% average accuracy with only 10% labeled target data on four real-world datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 17.24 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a practical pathway toward cost-effective predictive maintenance in Industry 4.0 applications.

title: A Fair Evaluation of Graph Foundation Models for Node Property Prediction
comment: Accepted at The Workshop on Graph Foundation Models at ICML 2026
summary: Due to the wide use of graph-structured data in different fields of industry and science, the development of Graph Foundation Models (GFMs) has recently attracted a lot of attention. While many different types of models are called GFMs, particular interest has been paid to GFMs designed for node property prediction tasks, which is one of the most popular settings in Graph ML with lots of real-world applications from fraud detection in financial and social networks to recommendation systems for e-commerce and user-generated content platforms. While a number of GFMs for this task have been recently proposed, the field has not converged to a unified evaluation setting, and different works evaluate their models in widely different ways, preventing reliable comparison of GFMs with each other and with other types of models. In this work, we conduct a fair and rigorous reevaluation of 9 recent GFMs for node property prediction, comparing them to strong Graph Neural Network (GNN) baselines. We find that, among these GFMs, only the most recent ones based on the Prior-data Fitted Networks paradigm outperform well-tuned GNNs in predictive performance, although at a higher inference cost.

AI.tldr: This paper reevaluates 9 recent Graph Foundation Models (GFMs) for node property prediction under a unified, fair setting and finds that only the most recent Prior-data Fitted Networks-based GFMs outperform well-tuned GNNs, albeit with higher inference cost.
AI.motivation: The field of GFMs for node property prediction lacks a unified evaluation setting, making it difficult to reliably compare different models. This work aims to address this gap by conducting a fair and rigorous reevaluation.
AI.method: The authors conduct a controlled empirical study comparing 9 recent GFMs against strong Graph Neural Network (GNN) baselines using a consistent evaluation setting.
AI.result: Only the most recent GFMs based on the Prior-data Fitted Networks paradigm outperform well-tuned GNNs in predictive performance, but at a higher inference cost.
AI.conclusion: The field has not converged; most GFMs do not surpass strong GNN baselines when fairly compared, highlighting the need for standardized evaluation protocols.

title: Reasoning as Attractor Dynamics: Latent Memory Retrieval via Gibbs-Weighted Energy Minimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are traditionally viewed as autoregressive generators. However, from the perspective of collective computation, they function as high-dimensional Dense Associative Memories that store complex reasoning patterns as latent attractors. In this work, we investigate the energy landscape of mathematical reasoning. We posit that correct reasoning chains correspond to deep, wide attractor basins ("flat minima") in the model's output distribution, whereas hallucinations manifest as sharp, unstable local minima. To exploit this geometry, we introduce a retrieval mechanism based on a Gibbs measure of the trajectory's spectral entropy. By sampling multiple reasoning paths and weighting them by their inverse energy ($P \propto e^{-βE}$), we approximate the equilibrium distribution of the associative memory, effectively ``relaxing'' the system into a robust solution. Empirically, this physics-inspired mechanism improves Microsoft Phi-3.5 performance on GSM8K by 5.38\% (84.7\% $\to$ 90.1\%), demonstrating that inference is better modeled as a dynamic settling process into an attractor basin rather than greedy next-token prediction.

AI.tldr: LLMs are reframed as Dense Associative Memories where correct reasoning corresponds to wide attractor basins (flat minima) and hallucinations to sharp minima. The authors propose a Gibbs-sampling-based retrieval mechanism using spectral entropy to weight reasoning paths by inverse energy, improving Phi-3.5 on GSM8K by 5.38% (84.7% → 90.1%).
